3: <header>1. Introduction </header>
The three basic challenges that the Livestock production sector faces globally are (MacLeod et al., 2013): a) to increase its pro- duction in order to cover global demand, b) to adapt to a con-

tinuously changing economic and natural environment and c) to reduce its environmental impact. A 35.3% of the global meat pro- duction can be attributed to the pig producing sector, rendering it the second most important contributor after the poultry sector (FAOSTAT, 2019). Thus, the global pig sector's share in confronting these challenges is of increased importance.
Pig production also comprises a dynamic sub-sector of the Live- stock production sector in Greece. Its production value at pro-

ducer price was 214.92 million Euro (17.6% of the total of ani- mal production in the country), being economically the second most important sub-sector of livestock production in the country, behind small ruminants' production (year 2018; Eurostat, 2019a). Epirus is one of the most densely pig populated regions in Greece. The specialist pig farms in the Region of Epirus pro- duced 29350 livestock units of pigs (19.67% of the total live- stock units of pigs in the country) with a standard output of 24.39 million Euro (19.42% of the total standard output of spe- cialist pig farms in the country), rendering this region the most important one in Greece in terms of pig production (year 2016; Eurostat, 2019b). Pig farms in this area were characterized by an intensification trend in the last decade (ELSTAT, 2018). Intensive pig farming has been conceded as an important source of pollu- tion due to the large manure quantities generated and due to the large quantities of grains utilized in pig rations (Cherubini et al., 2015).
As a contributor to air pollutants, pig production was respon- sible for the 5.8% and the 1.9% of the CH4 and N20 emissions

from the country's Agriculture Sector, respectively, being the fifth most important livestock category for greenhouse gas emissions in the country (after sheep, goats, non-dairy cattle and dairy cat- tle) (year 2017; HMEE (2019a)). Moreover, the management sys- tems of pig manure were responsible for the 5.9%, 0.02%, 3.9%, 0.2% and 1.0% of the country's Agricultural Sector's NH3, NO2, NMVOCs, PM2.5, PM10 and TSP emissions, being the third most important contributor to NH3 emissions (after non-dairy cattle and sheep) and less important for the rest of the emitted substances (year 2017; HMEE (2019b)). Although not the most important emitters, pig production systems in Greece have great potential in contribut- ing to the reduction of country's Agriculture Sector's NH3, CH4 and N20 emissions.
The increase in N use efficiency by improvement of livestock
performance has been suggested as a strategy for decreasing the
quantity of N excreted by livestock (UNECE, 2015) and as a conse-
quence the emitted N20 and NH3 at farm level.

In recent decades, research has suggested that addition of clay minerals in livestock rations enhances their feed efficiency (Parisini et al., 1999; Zhou et al., 2014). Attapulgite clay's use as a feed supplement to monogastric animals, in particular, has attracted the interest of several researchers in Greece (e.g. Kanoulas et al., 2017; Skoufos et al., 2016; Tzora et al., 2016). Kanoulas et al. (2017) suggested an improvement of some sow per- formance parameters (i.e. litter size 24 h postpartum, litter size at weaning, piglet weight at weaning and piglet average daily gain during lactation), when attapulgite was supplemented in sow ges- tation and lactation diets (at 0.7% w/w level). Furthermore, supple- mentation of weaned piglets' diets with a low dose (2 g/kg) of at- tapulgite was suggested to increase their growth performance, feed efficiency and nutrient digestibility in comparison to the basal ra- tion (Lv et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). Zhang et al. (2013) sug- gested a 21% increase in the average daily weight gain, a 7.3% in- crease in the average daily feed intake and a 11.2% decrease in the feed to gain ratio of the piglets. Moreover, an increase of 2.9%, 2.0% and 2.9% was found in the apparent total tract digestibility of dry matter (DM), gross energy (GE) and crude protein (CP), respectively (Lv et al., 2015).

Organic acids are added in monogastric animal diets as an alternative for antimicrobial growth promoters (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Tzora et al., 2016), after the ban of the latter at the EU level in 2003. Benzoic acid, specifically, has been approved in the EU for use in fattening pig diets since 2007 (at a level between 0.5 and 1%) (Malagutti et al., 2010) in Group M under the 70/524/EEC reg- ulation and for use in the compound feed industry. Some studies have proposed a positive effect on growth performance and feed efficiency in fattening pigs when benzoic acid was added up to a level of 1% in comparison to a basal diet (Van der Peet-Schwering et al., 1999; Zhai et al., 2017), while others a no significant effect (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Malagutti et al., 2010). More in detail, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed a 5.7% increase in average daily weight gain, a 2.0% increase in the average daily feed intake and a 4.0% decrease in the feed conversion ratio (FCR) with a 1% addition of benzoic acid, while Zhai et al. (2017) a 5.9% in- crease, a 2.6% increase and 3.0% decrease for the same performance attributes with a 0.3% addition of benzoic acid. Moreover, benzoic acid at this level has been shown to contribute to decreased urinary and total N excretion as well as manure NH3 emissions from fattening pigs. A 12.3% decrease in the total N excretion and a 30% decrease in the NH3 emission during manure storage was found by supplementing 1% of benzoic acid (Murphy et al., 2011).

Thus, examining the combined addition of attapulgite clay and benzoic acid in fattening pigs' diets as a practice for improving the environmental performance of pig production systems could be reasonable. However, to our knowledge, no information is at
the moment available about the effect of such an addition on the growth performance and nutrient digestibility and excretion in fat- tening pigs.

Nevertheless, studying exclusively the effect of a feeding prac- tice on the emissions of pollutants from a pig farm is not consid- ered adequate for assessing its effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Various stud- ies have suggested that the contribution of feed production to sev- eral environmental impacts associated with the pig meat supply chain is higher than the respective contribution of the pig farm (e.g. Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; González-García et al., 2015). An environmental Life Cycle Assessment (ELCA) approach is therefore appropriate for assessing the effect of a feeding practice on the environmental performance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Guinée et al. (2002) defined ELCA as a 'tool for the analysis of the environmental burden (i.e. resource use and emissions to the environment) of products at all stages in their life cycle from the extraction of resources, through the produc- tion of materials, product parts and the product itself, and the use of the product to the management after it is discarded, ei- ther by reuse, recycling or final disposal'. ELCA is considered as the most reliable methodology for environmental impact assess- ment which is able to analyze the environmental burden of prod- ucts at all stages in their life cycle and provide environmentally- friendly alternative solutions (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016).

A number of LCA studies have already dealt with the ef- fect of feeding practices on the environmental impact of pig meat production. Some of them have examined the effect of supplying growing and finishing pigs as well as gestating and lactating SOWS with diets of constant or variable protein lev- els and sources (e.g. Reckmann et al., 2016; van Zanten et al., 2015). Other studies examined the effect of supplementing di- ets of growing and finishing pigs with additives such as syn- thetic amino acids (testing various crude protein levels and sources) (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Ogino et al., 2013) and di- gestibility improving enzymes (Nielsen et al., 2008). In addition, Mackenzie et al. (2016) and Ali et al. (2017) studied the poten- tial for reducing the environmental impacts of pig production by including co-products of the food industry to the diets of grow- ing and finishing pigs. The study of Monteiro et al. (2017) fur- ther based its LCA on a specifically designed experimental pro- cedure for assessing the effect of two different feeding strategies (the first formulated to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess and the second with standard Brazilian rec- ommendations) on the growth performance of growing-finishing pigs.

To the best of our knowledge, few LCA studies for Greek agricultural, livestock or food products have been published (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Tsarouhas et al., 2015), with the studies of Anestis et al. (2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

The aim of this work was to study the effect of including at- tapulgite clay and benzoic acid in the ration of fattening pigs of a commercial pig farm located in the Region of Epirus, on the potential environmental impact of its production. To this pur- pose, an ELCA approach was considered. The current LCA uti- lized the fattening pig performance results of an in situ experi- mental procedure for examining two feeding practices: the con- ventional diet (CNVD) used by the farm and the CNVD supple- mented with attapulgite clay and benzoic acid (ATTBAD) at lev- els less than 1% w/w. This study further aims to contribute with its results to the limited existing LCA research on Greek livestock production.

(2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

ducer price was 214.92 million Euro (17.6% of the total of ani- mal production in the country), being economically the second most important sub-sector of livestock production in the country, behind small ruminants' production (year 2018; Eurostat, 2019a). Epirus is one of the most densely pig populated regions in Greece. The specialist pig farms in the Region of Epirus pro- duced 29350 livestock units of pigs (19.67% of the total live- stock units of pigs in the country) with a standard output of 24.39 million Euro (19.42% of the total standard output of spe- cialist pig farms in the country), rendering this region the most important one in Greece in terms of pig production (year 2016; Eurostat, 2019b). Pig farms in this area were characterized by an intensification trend in the last decade (ELSTAT, 2018). Intensive pig farming has been conceded as an important source of pollu- tion due to the large manure quantities generated and due to the large quantities of grains utilized in pig rations (Cherubini et al., 2015).
As a contributor to air pollutants, pig production was respon- sible for the 5.8% and the 1.9% of the CH4 and N20 emissions

from the country's Agriculture Sector, respectively, being the fifth most important livestock category for greenhouse gas emissions in the country (after sheep, goats, non-dairy cattle and dairy cat- tle) (year 2017; HMEE (2019a)). Moreover, the management sys- tems of pig manure were responsible for the 5.9%, 0.02%, 3.9%, 0.2% and 1.0% of the country's Agricultural Sector's NH3, NO2, NMVOCs, PM2.5, PM10 and TSP emissions, being the third most important contributor to NH3 emissions (after non-dairy cattle and sheep) and less important for the rest of the emitted substances (year 2017; HMEE (2019b)). Although not the most important emitters, pig production systems in Greece have great potential in contribut- ing to the reduction of country's Agriculture Sector's NH3, CH4 and N20 emissions.
The increase in N use efficiency by improvement of livestock
performance has been suggested as a strategy for decreasing the
quantity of N excreted by livestock (UNECE, 2015) and as a conse-
quence the emitted N20 and NH3 at farm level.

In recent decades, research has suggested that addition of clay minerals in livestock rations enhances their feed efficiency (Parisini et al., 1999; Zhou et al., 2014). Attapulgite clay's use as a feed supplement to monogastric animals, in particular, has attracted the interest of several researchers in Greece (e.g. Kanoulas et al., 2017; Skoufos et al., 2016; Tzora et al., 2016). Kanoulas et al. (2017) suggested an improvement of some sow per- formance parameters (i.e. litter size 24 h postpartum, litter size at weaning, piglet weight at weaning and piglet average daily gain during lactation), when attapulgite was supplemented in sow ges- tation and lactation diets (at 0.7% w/w level). Furthermore, supple- mentation of weaned piglets' diets with a low dose (2 g/kg) of at- tapulgite was suggested to increase their growth performance, feed efficiency and nutrient digestibility in comparison to the basal ra- tion (Lv et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). Zhang et al. (2013) sug- gested a 21% increase in the average daily weight gain, a 7.3% in- crease in the average daily feed intake and a 11.2% decrease in the feed to gain ratio of the piglets. Moreover, an increase of 2.9%, 2.0% and 2.9% was found in the apparent total tract digestibility of dry matter (DM), gross energy (GE) and crude protein (CP), respectively (Lv et al., 2015).

Organic acids are added in monogastric animal diets as an alternative for antimicrobial growth promoters (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Tzora et al., 2016), after the ban of the latter at the EU level in 2003. Benzoic acid, specifically, has been approved in the EU for use in fattening pig diets since 2007 (at a level between 0.5 and 1%) (Malagutti et al., 2010) in Group M under the 70/524/EEC reg- ulation and for use in the compound feed industry. Some studies have proposed a positive effect on growth performance and feed efficiency in fattening pigs when benzoic acid was added up to a level of 1% in comparison to a basal diet (Van der Peet-Schwering et al., 1999; Zhai et al., 2017), while others a no significant effect (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Malagutti et al., 2010). More in detail, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed a 5.7% increase in average daily weight gain, a 2.0% increase in the average daily feed intake and a 4.0% decrease in the feed conversion ratio (FCR) with a 1% addition of benzoic acid, while Zhai et al. (2017) a 5.9% in- crease, a 2.6% increase and 3.0% decrease for the same performance attributes with a 0.3% addition of benzoic acid. Moreover, benzoic acid at this level has been shown to contribute to decreased urinary and total N excretion as well as manure NH3 emissions from fattening pigs. A 12.3% decrease in the total N excretion and a 30% decrease in the NH3 emission during manure storage was found by supplementing 1% of benzoic acid (Murphy et al., 2011).

Thus, examining the combined addition of attapulgite clay and benzoic acid in fattening pigs' diets as a practice for improving the environmental performance of pig production systems could be reasonable. However, to our knowledge, no information is at
the moment available about the effect of such an addition on the growth performance and nutrient digestibility and excretion in fat- tening pigs.

Nevertheless, studying exclusively the effect of a feeding prac- tice on the emissions of pollutants from a pig farm is not consid- ered adequate for assessing its effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Various stud- ies have suggested that the contribution of feed production to sev- eral environmental impacts associated with the pig meat supply chain is higher than the respective contribution of the pig farm (e.g. Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; González-García et al., 2015). An environmental Life Cycle Assessment (ELCA) approach is therefore appropriate for assessing the effect of a feeding practice on the environmental performance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Guinée et al. (2002) defined ELCA as a 'tool for the analysis of the environmental burden (i.e. resource use and emissions to the environment) of products at all stages in their life cycle from the extraction of resources, through the produc- tion of materials, product parts and the product itself, and the use of the product to the management after it is discarded, ei- ther by reuse, recycling or final disposal'. ELCA is considered as the most reliable methodology for environmental impact assess- ment which is able to analyze the environmental burden of prod- ucts at all stages in their life cycle and provide environmentally- friendly alternative solutions (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016).

A number of LCA studies have already dealt with the ef- fect of feeding practices on the environmental impact of pig meat production. Some of them have examined the effect of supplying growing and finishing pigs as well as gestating and lactating SOWS with diets of constant or variable protein lev- els and sources (e.g. Reckmann et al., 2016; van Zanten et al., 2015). Other studies examined the effect of supplementing di- ets of growing and finishing pigs with additives such as syn- thetic amino acids (testing various crude protein levels and sources) (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Ogino et al., 2013) and di- gestibility improving enzymes (Nielsen et al., 2008). In addition, Mackenzie et al. (2016) and Ali et al. (2017) studied the poten- tial for reducing the environmental impacts of pig production by including co-products of the food industry to the diets of grow- ing and finishing pigs. The study of Monteiro et al. (2017) fur- ther based its LCA on a specifically designed experimental pro- cedure for assessing the effect of two different feeding strategies (the first formulated to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess and the second with standard Brazilian rec- ommendations) on the growth performance of growing-finishing pigs.

To the best of our knowledge, few LCA studies for Greek agricultural, livestock or food products have been published (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Tsarouhas et al., 2015), with the studies of Anestis et al. (2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

The aim of this work was to study the effect of including at- tapulgite clay and benzoic acid in the ration of fattening pigs of a commercial pig farm located in the Region of Epirus, on the potential environmental impact of its production. To this pur- pose, an ELCA approach was considered. The current LCA uti- lized the fattening pig performance results of an in situ experi- mental procedure for examining two feeding practices: the con- ventional diet (CNVD) used by the farm and the CNVD supple- mented with attapulgite clay and benzoic acid (ATTBAD) at lev- els less than 1% w/w. This study further aims to contribute with its results to the limited existing LCA research on Greek livestock production.

Other studies examined the effect of supplementing di- ets of growing and finishing pigs with additives such as syn- thetic amino acids (testing various crude protein levels and sources) (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Ogino et al., 2013) and di- gestibility improving enzymes (Nielsen et al., 2008). In addition, Mackenzie et al. (2016) and Ali et al. (2017) studied the poten- tial for reducing the environmental impacts of pig production by including co-products of the food industry to the diets of grow- ing and finishing pigs. The study of Monteiro et al. (2017) fur- ther based its LCA on a specifically designed experimental pro- cedure for assessing the effect of two different feeding strategies (the first formulated to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess and the second with standard Brazilian rec- ommendations) on the growth performance of growing-finishing pigs.

To the best of our knowledge, few LCA studies for Greek agricultural, livestock or food products have been published (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Tsarouhas et al., 2015), with the studies of Anestis et al. (2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

The aim of this work was to study the effect of including at- tapulgite clay and benzoic acid in the ration of fattening pigs of a commercial pig farm located in the Region of Epirus, on the potential environmental impact of its production. To this pur- pose, an ELCA approach was considered. The current LCA uti- lized the fattening pig performance results of an in situ experi- mental procedure for examining two feeding practices: the con- ventional diet (CNVD) used by the farm and the CNVD supple- mented with attapulgite clay and benzoic acid (ATTBAD) at lev- els less than 1% w/w. This study further aims to contribute with its results to the limited existing LCA research on Greek livestock production.

ducer price was 214.92 million Euro (17.6% of the total of ani- mal production in the country), being economically the second most important sub-sector of livestock production in the country, behind small ruminants' production (year 2018; Eurostat, 2019a). Epirus is one of the most densely pig populated regions in Greece. The specialist pig farms in the Region of Epirus pro- duced 29350 livestock units of pigs (19.67% of the total live- stock units of pigs in the country) with a standard output of 24.39 million Euro (19.42% of the total standard output of spe- cialist pig farms in the country), rendering this region the most important one in Greece in terms of pig production (year 2016; Eurostat, 2019b). Pig farms in this area were characterized by an intensification trend in the last decade (ELSTAT, 2018). Intensive pig farming has been conceded as an important source of pollu- tion due to the large manure quantities generated and due to the large quantities of grains utilized in pig rations (Cherubini et al., 2015).
As a contributor to air pollutants, pig production was respon- sible for the 5.8% and the 1.9% of the CH4 and N20 emissions

from the country's Agriculture Sector, respectively, being the fifth most important livestock category for greenhouse gas emissions in the country (after sheep, goats, non-dairy cattle and dairy cat- tle) (year 2017; HMEE (2019a)). Moreover, the management sys- tems of pig manure were responsible for the 5.9%, 0.02%, 3.9%, 0.2% and 1.0% of the country's Agricultural Sector's NH3, NO2, NMVOCs, PM2.5, PM10 and TSP emissions, being the third most important contributor to NH3 emissions (after non-dairy cattle and sheep) and less important for the rest of the emitted substances (year 2017; HMEE (2019b)). Although not the most important emitters, pig production systems in Greece have great potential in contribut- ing to the reduction of country's Agriculture Sector's NH3, CH4 and N20 emissions.
The increase in N use efficiency by improvement of livestock
performance has been suggested as a strategy for decreasing the
quantity of N excreted by livestock (UNECE, 2015) and as a conse-
quence the emitted N20 and NH3 at farm level.

In recent decades, research has suggested that addition of clay minerals in livestock rations enhances their feed efficiency (Parisini et al., 1999; Zhou et al., 2014). Attapulgite clay's use as a feed supplement to monogastric animals, in particular, has attracted the interest of several researchers in Greece (e.g. Kanoulas et al., 2017; Skoufos et al., 2016; Tzora et al., 2016). Kanoulas et al. (2017) suggested an improvement of some sow per- formance parameters (i.e. litter size 24 h postpartum, litter size at weaning, piglet weight at weaning and piglet average daily gain during lactation), when attapulgite was supplemented in sow ges- tation and lactation diets (at 0.7% w/w level). Furthermore, supple- mentation of weaned piglets' diets with a low dose (2 g/kg) of at- tapulgite was suggested to increase their growth performance, feed efficiency and nutrient digestibility in comparison to the basal ra- tion (Lv et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). Zhang et al. (2013) sug- gested a 21% increase in the average daily weight gain, a 7.3% in- crease in the average daily feed intake and a 11.2% decrease in the feed to gain ratio of the piglets. Moreover, an increase of 2.9%, 2.0% and 2.9% was found in the apparent total tract digestibility of dry matter (DM), gross energy (GE) and crude protein (CP), respectively (Lv et al., 2015).

Organic acids are added in monogastric animal diets as an alternative for antimicrobial growth promoters (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Tzora et al., 2016), after the ban of the latter at the EU level in 2003. Benzoic acid, specifically, has been approved in the EU for use in fattening pig diets since 2007 (at a level between 0.5 and 1%) (Malagutti et al., 2010) in Group M under the 70/524/EEC reg- ulation and for use in the compound feed industry. Some studies have proposed a positive effect on growth performance and feed efficiency in fattening pigs when benzoic acid was added up to a level of 1% in comparison to a basal diet (Van der Peet-Schwering et al., 1999; Zhai et al., 2017), while others a no significant effect (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Malagutti et al., 2010). More in detail, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed a 5.7% increase in average daily weight gain, a 2.0% increase in the average daily feed intake and a 4.0% decrease in the feed conversion ratio (FCR) with a 1% addition of benzoic acid, while Zhai et al. (2017) a 5.9% in- crease, a 2.6% increase and 3.0% decrease for the same performance attributes with a 0.3% addition of benzoic acid. Moreover, benzoic acid at this level has been shown to contribute to decreased urinary and total N excretion as well as manure NH3 emissions from fattening pigs. A 12.3% decrease in the total N excretion and a 30% decrease in the NH3 emission during manure storage was found by supplementing 1% of benzoic acid (Murphy et al., 2011).

Thus, examining the combined addition of attapulgite clay and benzoic acid in fattening pigs' diets as a practice for improving the environmental performance of pig production systems could be reasonable. However, to our knowledge, no information is at
the moment available about the effect of such an addition on the growth performance and nutrient digestibility and excretion in fat- tening pigs.

Nevertheless, studying exclusively the effect of a feeding prac- tice on the emissions of pollutants from a pig farm is not consid- ered adequate for assessing its effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Various stud- ies have suggested that the contribution of feed production to sev- eral environmental impacts associated with the pig meat supply chain is higher than the respective contribution of the pig farm (e.g. Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; González-García et al., 2015). An environmental Life Cycle Assessment (ELCA) approach is therefore appropriate for assessing the effect of a feeding practice on the environmental performance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Guinée et al. (2002) defined ELCA as a 'tool for the analysis of the environmental burden (i.e. resource use and emissions to the environment) of products at all stages in their life cycle from the extraction of resources, through the produc- tion of materials, product parts and the product itself, and the use of the product to the management after it is discarded, ei- ther by reuse, recycling or final disposal'. ELCA is considered as the most reliable methodology for environmental impact assess- ment which is able to analyze the environmental burden of prod- ucts at all stages in their life cycle and provide environmentally- friendly alternative solutions (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016).

In addition, Mackenzie et al. (2016) and Ali et al. (2017) studied the poten- tial for reducing the environmental impacts of pig production by including co-products of the food industry to the diets of grow- ing and finishing pigs. The study of Monteiro et al. (2017) fur- ther based its LCA on a specifically designed experimental pro- cedure for assessing the effect of two different feeding strategies (the first formulated to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess and the second with standard Brazilian rec- ommendations) on the growth performance of growing-finishing pigs.

To the best of our knowledge, few LCA studies for Greek agricultural, livestock or food products have been published (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Tsarouhas et al., 2015), with the studies of Anestis et al. (2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

The aim of this work was to study the effect of including at- tapulgite clay and benzoic acid in the ration of fattening pigs of a commercial pig farm located in the Region of Epirus, on the potential environmental impact of its production. This study further aims to contribute with its results to the limited existing LCA research on Greek livestock production.

(2002) defined ELCA as a 'tool for the analysis of the environmental burden (i.e. resource use and emissions to the environment) of products at all stages in their life cycle from the extraction of resources, through the produc- tion of materials, product parts and the product itself, and the use of the product to the management after it is discarded, ei- ther by reuse, recycling or final disposal'. ELCA is considered as the most reliable methodology for environmental impact assess- ment which is able to analyze the environmental burden of prod- ucts at all stages in their life cycle and provide environmentally- friendly alternative solutions (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016).

A number of LCA studies have already dealt with the ef- fect of feeding practices on the environmental impact of pig meat production. Some of them have examined the effect of supplying growing and finishing pigs as well as gestating and lactating SOWS with diets of constant or variable protein lev- els and sources (e.g. Reckmann et al., 2016; van Zanten et al., 2015). Other studies examined the effect of supplementing di- ets of growing and finishing pigs with additives such as syn- thetic amino acids (testing various crude protein levels and sources) (Garcia-Launay et al., 2014; Ogino et al., 2013) and di- gestibility improving enzymes (Nielsen et al., 2008). In addition, Mackenzie et al. (2016) and Ali et al. (2017) studied the poten- tial for reducing the environmental impacts of pig production by including co-products of the food industry to the diets of grow- ing and finishing pigs. The study of Monteiro et al. (2017) fur- ther based its LCA on a specifically designed experimental pro- cedure for assessing the effect of two different feeding strategies (the first formulated to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess and the second with standard Brazilian rec- ommendations) on the growth performance of growing-finishing pigs.

To the best of our knowledge, few LCA studies for Greek agricultural, livestock or food products have been published (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Tsarouhas et al., 2015), with the studies of Anestis et al. (2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

The aim of this work was to study the effect of including at- tapulgite clay and benzoic acid in the ration of fattening pigs of a commercial pig farm located in the Region of Epirus, on the potential environmental impact of its production. To this pur- pose, an ELCA approach was considered. The current LCA uti- lized the fattening pig performance results of an in situ experi- mental procedure for examining two feeding practices: the con- ventional diet (CNVD) used by the farm and the CNVD supple- mented with attapulgite clay and benzoic acid (ATTBAD) at lev- els less than 1% w/w. This study further aims to contribute with its results to the limited existing LCA research on Greek livestock production.

ducer price was 214.92 million Euro (17.6% of the total of ani- mal production in the country), being economically the second most important sub-sector of livestock production in the country, behind small ruminants' production (year 2018; Eurostat, 2019a). Epirus is one of the most densely pig populated regions in Greece. The specialist pig farms in the Region of Epirus pro- duced 29350 livestock units of pigs (19.67% of the total live- stock units of pigs in the country) with a standard output of 24.39 million Euro (19.42% of the total standard output of spe- cialist pig farms in the country), rendering this region the most important one in Greece in terms of pig production (year 2016; Eurostat, 2019b). Pig farms in this area were characterized by an intensification trend in the last decade (ELSTAT, 2018). Intensive pig farming has been conceded as an important source of pollu- tion due to the large manure quantities generated and due to the large quantities of grains utilized in pig rations (Cherubini et al., 2015).
As a contributor to air pollutants, pig production was respon- sible for the 5.8% and the 1.9% of the CH4 and N20 emissions

from the country's Agriculture Sector, respectively, being the fifth most important livestock category for greenhouse gas emissions in the country (after sheep, goats, non-dairy cattle and dairy cat- tle) (year 2017; HMEE (2019a)). Moreover, the management sys- tems of pig manure were responsible for the 5.9%, 0.02%, 3.9%, 0.2% and 1.0% of the country's Agricultural Sector's NH3, NO2, NMVOCs, PM2.5, PM10 and TSP emissions, being the third most important contributor to NH3 emissions (after non-dairy cattle and sheep) and less important for the rest of the emitted substances (year 2017; HMEE (2019b)). Although not the most important emitters, pig production systems in Greece have great potential in contribut- ing to the reduction of country's Agriculture Sector's NH3, CH4 and N20 emissions.
The increase in N use efficiency by improvement of livestock
performance has been suggested as a strategy for decreasing the
quantity of N excreted by livestock (UNECE, 2015) and as a conse-
quence the emitted N20 and NH3 at farm level.

In recent decades, research has suggested that addition of clay minerals in livestock rations enhances their feed efficiency (Parisini et al., 1999; Zhou et al., 2014). Attapulgite clay's use as a feed supplement to monogastric animals, in particular, has attracted the interest of several researchers in Greece (e.g. Kanoulas et al., 2017; Skoufos et al., 2016; Tzora et al., 2016). Kanoulas et al. (2017) suggested an improvement of some sow per- formance parameters (i.e. litter size 24 h postpartum, litter size at weaning, piglet weight at weaning and piglet average daily gain during lactation), when attapulgite was supplemented in sow ges- tation and lactation diets (at 0.7% w/w level). Furthermore, supple- mentation of weaned piglets' diets with a low dose (2 g/kg) of at- tapulgite was suggested to increase their growth performance, feed efficiency and nutrient digestibility in comparison to the basal ra- tion (Lv et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2013). Zhang et al. (2013) sug- gested a 21% increase in the average daily weight gain, a 7.3% in- crease in the average daily feed intake and a 11.2% decrease in the feed to gain ratio of the piglets. Moreover, an increase of 2.9%, 2.0% and 2.9% was found in the apparent total tract digestibility of dry matter (DM), gross energy (GE) and crude protein (CP), respectively (Lv et al., 2015).

Organic acids are added in monogastric animal diets as an alternative for antimicrobial growth promoters (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Tzora et al., 2016), after the ban of the latter at the EU level in 2003. Benzoic acid, specifically, has been approved in the EU for use in fattening pig diets since 2007 (at a level between 0.5 and 1%) (Malagutti et al., 2010) in Group M under the 70/524/EEC reg- ulation and for use in the compound feed industry. Some studies have proposed a positive effect on growth performance and feed efficiency in fattening pigs when benzoic acid was added up to a level of 1% in comparison to a basal diet (Van der Peet-Schwering et al., 1999; Zhai et al., 2017), while others a no significant effect (Giannenas et al., 2016a; Malagutti et al., 2010). More in detail, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed a 5.7% increase in average daily weight gain, a 2.0% increase in the average daily feed intake and a 4.0% decrease in the feed conversion ratio (FCR) with a 1% addition of benzoic acid, while Zhai et al. (2017) a 5.9% in- crease, a 2.6% increase and 3.0% decrease for the same performance attributes with a 0.3% addition of benzoic acid. Moreover, benzoic acid at this level has been shown to contribute to decreased urinary and total N excretion as well as manure NH3 emissions from fattening pigs. A 12.3% decrease in the total N excretion and a 30% decrease in the NH3 emission during manure storage was found by supplementing 1% of benzoic acid (Murphy et al., 2011).

Thus, examining the combined addition of attapulgite clay and benzoic acid in fattening pigs' diets as a practice for improving the environmental performance of pig production systems could be reasonable. However, to our knowledge, no information is at
the moment available about the effect of such an addition on the growth performance and nutrient digestibility and excretion in fat- tening pigs.

Nevertheless, studying exclusively the effect of a feeding prac- tice on the emissions of pollutants from a pig farm is not consid- ered adequate for assessing its effect on the environmental perfor- mance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Various stud- ies have suggested that the contribution of feed production to sev- eral environmental impacts associated with the pig meat supply chain is higher than the respective contribution of the pig farm (e.g. Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; González-García et al., 2015). An environmental Life Cycle Assessment (ELCA) approach is therefore appropriate for assessing the effect of a feeding practice on the environmental performance of the farm's product supplied to the market. Guinée et al. (2002) defined ELCA as a 'tool for the analysis of the environmental burden (i.e. resource use and emissions to the environment) of products at all stages in their life cycle from the extraction of resources, through the produc- tion of materials, product parts and the product itself, and the use of the product to the management after it is discarded, ei- ther by reuse, recycling or final disposal'. ELCA is considered as the most reliable methodology for environmental impact assess- ment which is able to analyze the environmental burden of prod- ucts at all stages in their life cycle and provide environmentally- friendly alternative solutions (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016).

In addition, Mackenzie et al. (2016) and Ali et al. (2017) studied the poten- tial for reducing the environmental impacts of pig production by including co-products of the food industry to the diets of grow- ing and finishing pigs. The study of Monteiro et al. (2017) fur- ther based its LCA on a specifically designed experimental pro- cedure for assessing the effect of two different feeding strategies (the first formulated to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess and the second with standard Brazilian rec- ommendations) on the growth performance of growing-finishing pigs.

To the best of our knowledge, few LCA studies for Greek agricultural, livestock or food products have been published (e.g. Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Tsarouhas et al., 2015), with the studies of Anestis et al. (2015) and Giannenas et al. (2017) being the only available relevant LCA research on livestock products, the former referring to dairy cattle and the latter to broiler production processes.

The aim of this work was to study the effect of including at- tapulgite clay and benzoic acid in the ration of fattening pigs of a commercial pig farm located in the Region of Epirus, on the potential environmental impact of its production. To this pur- pose, an ELCA approach was considered. The current LCA uti- lized the fattening pig performance results of an in situ experi- mental procedure for examining two feeding practices: the con- ventional diet (CNVD) used by the farm and the CNVD supple- mented with attapulgite clay and benzoic acid (ATTBAD) at lev- els less than 1% w/w. This study further aims to contribute with its results to the limited existing LCA research on Greek livestock production.

10: <header>2.1. Experimental procedure </header>

Pig farm. The experimental procedure took place in a commer- cial farrowing to finishing pig farm located close to Neos Oropos (39°08'27.3"N 20°44'34.2"E), a rural area in the Region of Epirus, Greece. The farm used the large-scale intensive type of production. The reared pig categories were housed in pens and in closed, me- chanically ventilated buildings with slatted floor. Electrical heaters were used for covering the animals' needs for heat in the farrow- ing and nursery houses. Pigs' excreta were handled as slurry which was stored in an open earthen pond located outside the pig build- ings (for a period of 4 to 6 months), where formation of natu- ral crust took place. All feed ingredients were produced off-farm. However, the various rations' formulation was performed on-farm. Water well was received by using a ground drilling installation.

Animals and housing. In this experiment, pigs were handled in compliance with the local and EU regulation for the protection of animals used for scientific purposes (Directive 2010/63/EU). In to- tal, 120 crossbred (1/4 Large White X 1/4 Landrace X 1/2 Duroc) male and female fattening pigs were involved. They were housed in the pens (6 m X 2.2 m) of the buildings of the commercial farm and fattened under the farm's standard husbandry conditions. Their access to feed and drinking water was ad libitum.

Dietary treatments. Two diets were examined (CNVD, ATTBAD Table 1). The CNVD was the diet supplied by the farmer and for- mulated based on maize, barley, wheat bran and soybean meal and according to the nutrient recommendations of NRC (2012). The AT- TBAD was the CNVD supplemented with 4 g/kg attapulgite (UI- traFed 75% attapulgite, Geohellas S.A.) and 5 g/kg benzoic acid (VevoVitall®, 100% benzoic acid, DSM Nutritional Products Ltd.) at the expense of maize grain. It was further isoenergetic and isoni- trogenous to the CNVD. Both diets were supplied in a meal form.

Experimental design. The duration of the experiment was equal to one fattening period (i.e. 59 days on average). The average age of the fattening pigs at the start and at the end of the experi- ment was 111 (+1) days and 170 (+1) days, respectively. The total number of fattening pigs was randomly allotted to two groups in accordance to the two dietary treatments. Each dietary treatment was tested in 4 replicate pens with 15 pigs per pen.

Measurements and statistical analysis. Fattening pigs were individually weighed on day 1 and day 59. Pen feed consump- tion was daily recorded during the experimental period. The daily weight gain (DWG) and the 59-d feed intake (FI) per head and the FCR were calculated by using these measurements.

The collected data was subjected to one-way ANOVA, using the IBM SPSS Statistics V. 20.0 Statistical Package (SPSS Inc., Chigaco, IL, USA). In every case the replication (pen) was used as the exper- imental unit. For the ANOVA, the groups of pigs were examined as fixed factors. The homogeneity of variances was examined with Levene's test (Levene, 1960). The level of significance was set at 5%.

All feed ingredients were produced off-farm. However, the various rations' formulation was performed on-farm. Water well was received by using a ground drilling installation.

Animals and housing. In this experiment, pigs were handled in compliance with the local and EU regulation for the protection of animals used for scientific purposes (Directive 2010/63/EU). In to- tal, 120 crossbred (1/4 Large White X 1/4 Landrace X 1/2 Duroc) male and female fattening pigs were involved. They were housed in the pens (6 m X 2.2 m) of the buildings of the commercial farm and fattened under the farm's standard husbandry conditions. Their access to feed and drinking water was ad libitum.

Dietary treatments. Two diets were examined (CNVD, ATTBAD Table 1). The CNVD was the diet supplied by the farmer and for- mulated based on maize, barley, wheat bran and soybean meal and according to the nutrient recommendations of NRC (2012). The AT- TBAD was the CNVD supplemented with 4 g/kg attapulgite (UI- traFed 75% attapulgite, Geohellas S.A.) and 5 g/kg benzoic acid (VevoVitall®, 100% benzoic acid, DSM Nutritional Products Ltd.) at the expense of maize grain. It was further isoenergetic and isoni- trogenous to the CNVD. Both diets were supplied in a meal form.

Experimental design. The duration of the experiment was equal to one fattening period (i.e. 59 days on average). The average age of the fattening pigs at the start and at the end of the experi- ment was 111 (+1) days and 170 (+1) days, respectively. The total number of fattening pigs was randomly allotted to two groups in accordance to the two dietary treatments. Each dietary treatment was tested in 4 replicate pens with 15 pigs per pen.

Measurements and statistical analysis. Fattening pigs were individually weighed on day 1 and day 59. Pen feed consump- tion was daily recorded during the experimental period. The daily weight gain (DWG) and the 59-d feed intake (FI) per head and the FCR were calculated by using these measurements.

The collected data was subjected to one-way ANOVA, using the IBM SPSS Statistics V. 20.0 Statistical Package (SPSS Inc., Chigaco, IL, USA). In every case the replication (pen) was used as the exper- imental unit. For the ANOVA, the groups of pigs were examined as fixed factors. The homogeneity of variances was examined with Levene's test (Levene, 1960). The level of significance was set at 5%.
13: <header>2.2. Environmental life cycle assessment </header>

Goal and scope definition. The evaluation of the environmental performance was realized by assessing the midpoint environmen- tal impacts of the systems associated with the experimental CNVD and ATTBAD and identifying the environmental hot spots of the re- spective pig supply chains. It was of interest to provide an entirely descriptive documentation of the systems under analysis (status- quo situation), with attributional modeling being the relevant LCA approach (EC-JRC, 2010).
The functional unit (FU) was defined as '1 kg of pig live-weight at the farm gate' and refers to the sum of the live-weight of the finished pigs and spent SOWS (FAO, 2018). A "cradle-to-farm-gate"

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-
