<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

analysis was performed (Fig. 1). Production and maintenance of infrastructure inputs (i.e. buildings and machinery) with a lifetime larger than 1 year, was excluded from the analysis (FAO, 2018). Pro- duction and transport processes of veterinary medicines and other chemicals (e.g. for cleaning and disinfection) were also excluded (e.g. González-García et al., 2015).

In the foreground system (Fig. 1), allocation of the environmental burden was not required, as one aggregated pig live-weight prod- uct outflow was considered and manure was treated as a resid- ual of the pig farming process, with no function within the system boundaries (FAO, 2018). An economic allocation approach (Supple- mentary material - Tables A1 and A2) was followed for the co- products of the processes in the background system (FAO, 2016).

Life Cycle Inventory Compilation. Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

Average zootechnical data, the % w/w composition of the rations for the various pig cohorts and the supplied ration quantities (Tables 2-4) as well as the daily supplies of drinking and cleaning water and the annual electricity supply, were collected after personal communication with the farm owner. The average live-weight of finished pigs at sale, the DWG of pigs and the FI per head in the fattening stage were received as a result of the dietary trials (Section 3.1).

A feed loss of 5% (Bartali et al., 1999) during consumption was assumed. An additional 2% w/w loss during the rations' formu- lation was considered for the purchased quantities of feed con- stituents.

Emission of CH4 from enteric fermentation was estimated by using the default Tier 1 IPCC emission factor (EF) (IPCC, 2006a). The default IPCC Tier 2 approach (FAO, 2018; IPCC, 2006a) was ap- plied for the emissions of CH4 and direct N20 from on-farm slurry management (OFSM). Furthermore, daily excreted manure proper- ties were taken from Bartali et al. (1999). Volatile solids (VS) from feed waste were also taken into account (FAO, 2018). ASAE Stan- dards were employed for quantifying N retention in SOWS and fat- teners (ASAE, 2005). According to the proposed methodology, N retention in gestating SOWS is a function of the gestation lean tis- sue gain (GLTG) and the number of piglets per parturition. N re- tention in lactating SOWS is a function of the lactation lean tissue gain (LLTG), the average weight of piglets at birth and the average weight of piglets at weaning. Furthermore, N retention in fattening pigs is dependent on the duration of the nursery phase, the ini- tial and final, average body weight of pigs in the nursery phase, the average fat-free lean gain (FFLG) from the start of the grow- ing period to the end of the fattening period, the body weight at the start of the growing period and the final (market) weight of pigs, the average dressing percent at the market weight and the av- erage fat-free lean percentage (FFLP) at market weight. For GLTG, LLTG and FFLG the default values available in ASAE (2005) were used. Indirect N20 emissions were estimated with the default Tier 1 IPCC EF (IPCC, 2006b, 2006a). The default EMEP/EEA Tier 2 ap- proach (EEA, 2016) was utilized for the evaluation of NH3 and NO2 emission as well as NMVOC emissions that occurred during hous- ing and slurry storage. Finally, PM1 and PM2.5 emissions were as- sessed with the default Tier 1 EMEP/EEA EFs (EEA, 2016). More information about the evaluation of annual on-farm emissions is provided in the Supplementary material (Table B1).

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible. These methods were generally in agree- ment with both the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Per- formance (LEAP) Partnership (FAO, 2018) and the EU's Product En-

<list>The domestic supplies of herring meal and of milk replacer solely consisted of imported products (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) 
Sweet whey was exclusively produced in the Greek Dairy Indus- try (FAOSTAT, 2018b, 2018c) (distance to the pig farm: 100 km). 
Electricity input to the pig farm was modeled by using the re- spective Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset. 
In the models of domestic cereal cultivation processes, the same types of pesticides' active substances and application rates per ha with the proprietary datasets were considered 
Electricity needs per kg of domestically produced mineral feed were presumed equal to the total energy needs per kg of compound feed produced for pigs (fatteners and sows) in the Netherlands (0.45 MJ/kg mineral feed, Vellinga et al., 2013) 
In mineral feeds' production, all ingredients except limestone, monocalcium phosphate and salt were assumed to be exclu- sively imported to the country, whereas those three ingredients to be produced in Greece 
The domestic supply mixes of wheat grain (wheat bran sup- ply chain) and soybeans (soybean meal and refined soybean oil supply chains) consisted of 37% and 100% imports, respectively (FAOSTAT, 2018a, 2018b) 
The imported fraction for the domestic supply of raw cow milk (sweet whey supply chain) was 19% (FAOSTAT, 2018c) 
Light fuel oil was considered as the basic source of the heat needed for domestic industrial operations (wheat milling, soy- bean crushing and dairy industry as well as quarries) 
Attapulgite production was modeled based on the Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset which refers to the quarry operation for bentonite due to lack of more relevant data. Bentonite clay was the most closely related mineral to attapulgite for which there was a data set available in SimaPro. 
Benzoic acid input to the pig farm was modeled by using the available global market Ecoinvent v.3.4. dataset for this chemi- cal, again due to lack of data more specific to this system. 
The solution mining process was used a proxy for domestic salt production (mineral feed supply chain), although salt produced by solar evaporation may be dominant in the Greek market (lack of relevant dataset).</list>

In addition, transport processes were considered in all supply chains. The datasets used for modeling the domestic production processes were adapted to the Greek context. For the material in- puts that imports were involved in their supply chains, a Greek market mix dataset was constructed.

The secondary LCI datasets used as a basis, the modifications applied and the elements of the constructed market mix datasets are also described in the Supplementary material (Tables C1 and C2). LCI modeling was realized in the SimaPro V. 8.5.2. PhD soft- ware.

Table 5 presents the inventory results per FU of the pig farm supplying the CNVD and ATTBAD to its pigs at the fattening stage. Impact Assessment. The environmental impact categories (EICs) addressed in this LCA were: climate change (CC) and CC from direct land use change (dLUC), acidification, eutrophication, land use, water use and fossil energy use. These constitute all the relevant midpoint EICs for pig supply chains (FAO, 2018, 2016). The methods utilized were for CC and CC from dLUC the 'IPCC 2013 GWP 100a V. 1.03', for acidification and eutrophication the 'ILCD 2011 Midpoint + V. 1.10', for land use the 'ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint (H) V. 1.02', for water use the 'AWARE V. 1.01' and for fossil en- ergy use the 'CML-IA baseline V. 3.05', as available in Pré (2017). The latter method was also used for acidification and eutrophi- cation assessment in order to make comparison with the litera- ture results more feasible.
