21: <header>3. Results and discussion </header>
3.1. Growth performance of fattening pigs
Table 6 presents the fattening pigs' performance parameters as a result of their supply with the CNVD and ATTBAD rations. The

pigs' live-weight at sale, the total weight gain (TWG) and the DWG in the fattening period were found significantly larger for the AT- TBAD (p < 0.05). Moreover, the FI and the FCR of the pigs in the fattening period were significantly lower (p < 0.05) when supplied the ATTBAD diet. There was no significant difference in the live- weight of the fatteners at the start of the fattening period when supplied the two diets. These results clearly show the improve- ment on performance parameters such as live body weight and feed efficiency in fattening pigs dietary supplemented with atta- pulgite and benzoic acid for a 59-day treatment.
The biological mechanisms in fattening pigs which explain this positive effect of the combined addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid on body weight gain and FCR remain to be studied.

Regarding the use of attapulgite, its physical properties could be used as a starting point in this respect. Giannenas et al. (2016b) ex- plained that attapulgite is a crystalloid hydrous magnesium- aluminum silicate mineral with a special laminated chain struc- ture in which crystalline lattice displacement exists. The crystals contain varying quantities of Na+, Ca2+, and and have the appearance as needles, fibers, or fibrous clusters. Attapulgite has very good colloidal properties with specific features, includ- ing dispersion. The modified attapulgite has much stronger absorp- tive features (the surface area has increased by 300 times more than common attapulgite), which could promote its functionality (Tang 2014). Kanoulas et al. (2017) used its high-water bind- ing capacity to make the hypothesis that attapulgite induced a de- creased gut transition rate and consequently a gradual release of nutrients in lactating sows, improving this way the mobilization of body reserves for supporting piglet growth up to weaning (associ- ated with a higher body weight loss from late gestation to piglet weaning for the SOWS fed with the standard diet supplemented with attapulgite in comparison to the SOWS fed with the standard diet). In their study, both the standard gestation and lactation di- ets of SOWS in two commercial farms in Greece (based on wheat grain, barley grain, wheat bran and soybean meal) were supple- mented with 7 g/kg of feed of attapulgite and with 8 g/kg of feed of an attapulgite blended product, for two consecutive reproductive phases. In the same study, the SOWS which were fed with the diets supplemented with attapulgite showed improved litter size at 24 postpartum, suggesting a possible positive influence of attapulgite on the embryo survival (improved embryonic and placental devel- opment during the early stages of gestation). They also showed an improved body weight gain of piglets during lactation.

The studies of Lv et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2013) reported an increase in the average daily weight gain and a decrease in the feed to body weight gain ratio of weaned piglets (up to the age of 66 days) supplied (for a 42-day period) with a control diet based on maize and soybean meal which was supplemented with 2 g/kg of diet of attapulgite at the expense of powdered rice hulls, in comparison to the control diet. The authors connected this im- provement in piglets' growth performance with increased trypsin activity in the duodenum and increased DM and GE digestibility. Lv et al. (2015) also reported an increase in CP digestibility and a decrease in the crude fat digestibility for the piglets fed the diet supplemented with attapulgite. The beneficial effect of attapulgite on intestinal integrity of the piglets (Zhang et al., 2013) was re- ported as a possible cause for improvement of the activity of di- gestive enzymes, enhancing DM, GE and CP utilization (Lv et al., 2015). Nevertheless, Lv et al. (2015) further reported negative ef- fects on DM, GE, CP and crude fat digestibility with an increase in attapulgite level from 2 g/kg to 3 g/kg of feed.

Furthermore, Chen et al. (2016) noticed inconsistent growth/laying performance results (positive or no effect) in literature dealing with poultry whose diets were supplemented with attapulgite at levels from 5 to 20 g/kg of diet and for trials' durations from 21 days to 24 weeks. They proposed that the effect

of attapulgite in poultry's growth performance is dependent on the species, the duration of supplementation, the dosage level and the variations in the attapulgite product used. Zhang et al. (2017) also noticed the aforementioned inconsistencies. However, their study indicated a significant increase in the average daily FI and daily weight gain in broilers (in the total 42-day experimental period) as well as a no significant influence to the FCR, when the basal starter and grower diets (based on maize and soybean meal) were supplemented with different levels of attapulgite (from 5 g/kg to 20 g/kg of diet). They explained this growth performance im- provement with the observed quality characteristics' enhancement of the supplied rations (hardness and durability index) after the addition of attapulgite and the increased FI. They also found a potential increase in N retention of broilers between 17-19 days of age and they attributed this to the observed increase in the digesta retention time (possible association with improved N utilization).

DM, CP, lipid and GE retention was also found to be increased in young pigs (12-35 kg of body weight) whose basal diet (based on cereal meals, cereal grains and protein meals of animal and veg- etable origin) was supplemented with 20 g/kg sepiolite, a clay with similar physical properties to attapulgite (Parisini et al., 1999). The authors suggested that for fatteners at this age, sepiolite could fa- vor digestive enzyme activity in the intestine as well as the bind- ing of harmful substances along the gastrointestinal tract. How- ever, no positive effect was observed on the growth performance (body weight gain and FCR) of either growing or fattening pigs (35-100 kg of body weight). Sepiolite has also been proposed to increase digesta retention time, allowing enzymes to provide ex- tended digestion activity of fat, protein and carbohydrates in the gut of pigs and poultry (Ouhida et al., 2000).

Furthermore, dietary inclusion of other clays, such as natural zeolites (e.g. clinoptilolite), have been suggested to promote the growth and nutrient retention in growing pigs (Papaioannou et al., 2002). More specifically, zeolites exert ion exchange, adsorption and catalytic properties, altering the gastrointestinal pH and the ionic composition of the lumen contents, thus affecting the enzy- matic activity of gastrointestinal secretions. Additionally, the well- known affinity of certain zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, for am- monium ions can favor the physiological status of the intesti- nal epithelium and thereby influence the digestive and absorp- tion processes (Papaioannou et al., 2002). It is also worthy of mentioning that natural clinoptilolite and its modified products have been proposed as antioxidants for broiler chicken tissues, enhancing health of broilers (Wu et al., 2013a, 2013b). However, Giannenas et al. (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

Regarding the effect of the addition of benzoic acid to the growth performance of fattening pigs, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) suggested a significant increase in average daily weight gain and daily FI and a significant improvement in FCR for fattening pigs from 43 kg of body weight to their market weight (108.9 kg), when their experimental diet was supplemented with 10 g/kg benzoic acid. In their experiment, supplementation of two different diets (starter and growing/finishing) with 10 g/kg benzoic acid took place for a period of 4 months (one fattening period; starter diet for 4 weeks, growing/finishing diet for the rest of the period), with an average starting weight of 24.3 kg/piglet. Both ex- perimental diets were based on cereal grains, tapioca and soybean meal. However, supplementation with 20 g/kg benzoic acid did not influence the growth performance (in comparison to the usual ex- perimental diet), suggesting the potential existence of an optimum level above which the growth performance is either not affected or decreased. The authors attributed this to a possible disturbance

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids. Moreover, the authors observed a higher total N and ammonium N excretion in the case of the diet supplemented with 20 g/kg benzoic acid. Nevertheless, this was connected to lower ammonia emission from the slurry pit due to the lower pH of the excreta achieved in the case of 20 g/kg benzoic acid.

Zhai et al. (2017) showed a linear increase in average daily weight gain, in FCR and in average daily FI of piglets in the nurs- ery stage when their basal diet (based on maize and soybean meal) was increasingly supplemented with 3 g/kg and 5 g/kg of benzoic acid, during a 28-day trial (average final body weight: 20 kg). Their findings were also similar for pigs in the growing and fattening stages, again for basal diets based on maize and soybean meal and for a 35-day experimental duration in each stage (initial and fi- nal average body weight: 36.1 kg and 112.4 kg). Possible reasons for this improvement in the growth performance of young piglets (nursery stage) which were discussed were the enhancement of the metabolic functions of the gut and the improvement of the ecosystem of microorganisms in the gut, the drop of pH of digesta in ileum, cecum and colon (associated to gut's health improve- ment), the higher activities of enzymes such as trypsin, lipase and amylase in the jejunum (associated to improvement in nutrient di- gestion and absorption) and the improved intestinal morphology, all caused by the addition of benzoic acid. For pigs in the grow- ing stage, an increase in the apparent P and Ca digestibility due to benzoic acid supplementation was discussed as a possible reason for the observed improvement in growth performance. They also suggested an optimal benzoic acid supplementation rate g/kg) for pigs in the growing and fattening stages and the studied pro- duction conditions, implying that growing, finishing pigs may per- form optimally with a lower level of benzoic acid than pigs in the nursery stage. Moreover, the results of Murphy et al. (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. The authors also observed a signifi- cant, linear increase in the ratio N retention to N intake. The total duration of the experimental procedure was 29 days. The reduc- tion in ammonia emission was explained by the decrease in the pH of the stored slurry, the lower availability of the total ammo- niacal nitrogen in the slurry and the increase in the ratio N re- tention to N intake, due to the supplementation of benzoic acid. The authors discussed that benzoic acid reduces urine pH and con- sequently slurry pH due to its conversion to hippuric acid in the liver, which is considered as one of the strongest acids excreted by the kidneys and causes urine acidification. Slurry pH influences the existing ammonia-ammonium cations equilibrium towards ammo- nium cations' production, reducing this way the release of ammo- nia gas from the slurry. They finally discussed that the increase in the ratio N retention to N intake could be caused by a decrease in the total aerobic bacteria number in the ileum, favoring ileal N di- gestibility, by a direct decrease in the pH in the gastric contents or by a reduction in the rate of gastric evacuation. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.

How- ever, no positive effect was observed on the growth performance (body weight gain and FCR) of either growing or fattening pigs (35-100 kg of body weight). Sepiolite has also been proposed to increase digesta retention time, allowing enzymes to provide ex- tended digestion activity of fat, protein and carbohydrates in the gut of pigs and poultry (Ouhida et al., 2000).

Furthermore, dietary inclusion of other clays, such as natural zeolites (e.g. clinoptilolite), have been suggested to promote the growth and nutrient retention in growing pigs (Papaioannou et al., 2002). More specifically, zeolites exert ion exchange, adsorption and catalytic properties, altering the gastrointestinal pH and the ionic composition of the lumen contents, thus affecting the enzy- matic activity of gastrointestinal secretions. Additionally, the well- known affinity of certain zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, for am- monium ions can favor the physiological status of the intesti- nal epithelium and thereby influence the digestive and absorp- tion processes (Papaioannou et al., 2002). It is also worthy of mentioning that natural clinoptilolite and its modified products have been proposed as antioxidants for broiler chicken tissues, enhancing health of broilers (Wu et al., 2013a, 2013b). However, Giannenas et al. (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

Regarding the effect of the addition of benzoic acid to the growth performance of fattening pigs, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) suggested a significant increase in average daily weight gain and daily FI and a significant improvement in FCR for fattening pigs from 43 kg of body weight to their market weight (108.9 kg), when their experimental diet was supplemented with 10 g/kg benzoic acid. In their experiment, supplementation of two different diets (starter and growing/finishing) with 10 g/kg benzoic acid took place for a period of 4 months (one fattening period; starter diet for 4 weeks, growing/finishing diet for the rest of the period), with an average starting weight of 24.3 kg/piglet. Both ex- perimental diets were based on cereal grains, tapioca and soybean meal. However, supplementation with 20 g/kg benzoic acid did not influence the growth performance (in comparison to the usual ex- perimental diet), suggesting the potential existence of an optimum level above which the growth performance is either not affected or decreased. The authors attributed this to a possible disturbance

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids. Moreover, the authors observed a higher total N and ammonium N excretion in the case of the diet supplemented with 20 g/kg benzoic acid. Nevertheless, this was connected to lower ammonia emission from the slurry pit due to the lower pH of the excreta achieved in the case of 20 g/kg benzoic acid.

Zhai et al. (2017) showed a linear increase in average daily weight gain, in FCR and in average daily FI of piglets in the nurs- ery stage when their basal diet (based on maize and soybean meal) was increasingly supplemented with 3 g/kg and 5 g/kg of benzoic acid, during a 28-day trial (average final body weight: 20 kg). Their findings were also similar for pigs in the growing and fattening stages, again for basal diets based on maize and soybean meal and for a 35-day experimental duration in each stage (initial and fi- nal average body weight: 36.1 kg and 112.4 kg). Possible reasons for this improvement in the growth performance of young piglets (nursery stage) which were discussed were the enhancement of the metabolic functions of the gut and the improvement of the ecosystem of microorganisms in the gut, the drop of pH of digesta in ileum, cecum and colon (associated to gut's health improve- ment), the higher activities of enzymes such as trypsin, lipase and amylase in the jejunum (associated to improvement in nutrient di- gestion and absorption) and the improved intestinal morphology, all caused by the addition of benzoic acid. For pigs in the grow- ing stage, an increase in the apparent P and Ca digestibility due to benzoic acid supplementation was discussed as a possible reason for the observed improvement in growth performance. They also suggested an optimal benzoic acid supplementation rate g/kg) for pigs in the growing and fattening stages and the studied pro- duction conditions, implying that growing, finishing pigs may per- form optimally with a lower level of benzoic acid than pigs in the nursery stage. Moreover, the results of Murphy et al. (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. The authors also observed a signifi- cant, linear increase in the ratio N retention to N intake. The total duration of the experimental procedure was 29 days. The reduc- tion in ammonia emission was explained by the decrease in the pH of the stored slurry, the lower availability of the total ammo- niacal nitrogen in the slurry and the increase in the ratio N re- tention to N intake, due to the supplementation of benzoic acid. The authors discussed that benzoic acid reduces urine pH and con- sequently slurry pH due to its conversion to hippuric acid in the liver, which is considered as one of the strongest acids excreted by the kidneys and causes urine acidification. Slurry pH influences the existing ammonia-ammonium cations equilibrium towards ammo- nium cations' production, reducing this way the release of ammo- nia gas from the slurry. They finally discussed that the increase in the ratio N retention to N intake could be caused by a decrease in the total aerobic bacteria number in the ileum, favoring ileal N di- gestibility, by a direct decrease in the pH in the gastric contents or by a reduction in the rate of gastric evacuation. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.
23: Table 6 presents the fattening pigs' performance parameters as a result of their supply with the CNVD and ATTBAD rations. The

pigs' live-weight at sale, the total weight gain (TWG) and the DWG in the fattening period were found significantly larger for the AT- TBAD (p < 0.05). Moreover, the FI and the FCR of the pigs in the fattening period were significantly lower (p < 0.05) when supplied the ATTBAD diet. There was no significant difference in the live- weight of the fatteners at the start of the fattening period when supplied the two diets. These results clearly show the improve- ment on performance parameters such as live body weight and feed efficiency in fattening pigs dietary supplemented with atta- pulgite and benzoic acid for a 59-day treatment.
The biological mechanisms in fattening pigs which explain this positive effect of the combined addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid on body weight gain and FCR remain to be studied.

Regarding the use of attapulgite, its physical properties could be used as a starting point in this respect. Giannenas et al. (2016b) ex- plained that attapulgite is a crystalloid hydrous magnesium- aluminum silicate mineral with a special laminated chain struc- ture in which crystalline lattice displacement exists. The crystals contain varying quantities of Na+, Ca2+, and and have the appearance as needles, fibers, or fibrous clusters. Attapulgite has very good colloidal properties with specific features, includ- ing dispersion. The modified attapulgite has much stronger absorp- tive features (the surface area has increased by 300 times more than common attapulgite), which could promote its functionality (Tang 2014). Kanoulas et al. (2017) used its high-water bind- ing capacity to make the hypothesis that attapulgite induced a de- creased gut transition rate and consequently a gradual release of nutrients in lactating sows, improving this way the mobilization of body reserves for supporting piglet growth up to weaning (associ- ated with a higher body weight loss from late gestation to piglet weaning for the SOWS fed with the standard diet supplemented with attapulgite in comparison to the SOWS fed with the standard diet). In their study, both the standard gestation and lactation di- ets of SOWS in two commercial farms in Greece (based on wheat grain, barley grain, wheat bran and soybean meal) were supple- mented with 7 g/kg of feed of attapulgite and with 8 g/kg of feed of an attapulgite blended product, for two consecutive reproductive phases. In the same study, the SOWS which were fed with the diets supplemented with attapulgite showed improved litter size at 24 postpartum, suggesting a possible positive influence of attapulgite on the embryo survival (improved embryonic and placental devel- opment during the early stages of gestation). They also showed an improved body weight gain of piglets during lactation.

The studies of Lv et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2013) reported an increase in the average daily weight gain and a decrease in the feed to body weight gain ratio of weaned piglets (up to the age of 66 days) supplied (for a 42-day period) with a control diet based on maize and soybean meal which was supplemented with 2 g/kg of diet of attapulgite at the expense of powdered rice hulls, in comparison to the control diet. The authors connected this im- provement in piglets' growth performance with increased trypsin activity in the duodenum and increased DM and GE digestibility. Lv et al. (2015) also reported an increase in CP digestibility and a decrease in the crude fat digestibility for the piglets fed the diet supplemented with attapulgite. The beneficial effect of attapulgite on intestinal integrity of the piglets (Zhang et al., 2013) was re- ported as a possible cause for improvement of the activity of di- gestive enzymes, enhancing DM, GE and CP utilization (Lv et al., 2015). Nevertheless, Lv et al. (2015) further reported negative ef- fects on DM, GE, CP and crude fat digestibility with an increase in attapulgite level from 2 g/kg to 3 g/kg of feed.

Furthermore, Chen et al. (2016) noticed inconsistent growth/laying performance results (positive or no effect) in literature dealing with poultry whose diets were supplemented with attapulgite at levels from 5 to 20 g/kg of diet and for trials' durations from 21 days to 24 weeks. They proposed that the effect

of attapulgite in poultry's growth performance is dependent on the species, the duration of supplementation, the dosage level and the variations in the attapulgite product used. Zhang et al. (2017) also noticed the aforementioned inconsistencies. However, their study indicated a significant increase in the average daily FI and daily weight gain in broilers (in the total 42-day experimental period) as well as a no significant influence to the FCR, when the basal starter and grower diets (based on maize and soybean meal) were supplemented with different levels of attapulgite (from 5 g/kg to 20 g/kg of diet). They explained this growth performance im- provement with the observed quality characteristics' enhancement of the supplied rations (hardness and durability index) after the addition of attapulgite and the increased FI. They also found a potential increase in N retention of broilers between 17-19 days of age and they attributed this to the observed increase in the digesta retention time (possible association with improved N utilization).

DM, CP, lipid and GE retention was also found to be increased in young pigs (12-35 kg of body weight) whose basal diet (based on cereal meals, cereal grains and protein meals of animal and veg- etable origin) was supplemented with 20 g/kg sepiolite, a clay with similar physical properties to attapulgite (Parisini et al., 1999). (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

Regarding the effect of the addition of benzoic acid to the growth performance of fattening pigs, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) suggested a significant increase in average daily weight gain and daily FI and a significant improvement in FCR for fattening pigs from 43 kg of body weight to their market weight (108.9 kg), when their experimental diet was supplemented with 10 g/kg benzoic acid. In their experiment, supplementation of two different diets (starter and growing/finishing) with 10 g/kg benzoic acid took place for a period of 4 months (one fattening period; starter diet for 4 weeks, growing/finishing diet for the rest of the period), with an average starting weight of 24.3 kg/piglet. Both ex- perimental diets were based on cereal grains, tapioca and soybean meal. However, supplementation with 20 g/kg benzoic acid did not influence the growth performance (in comparison to the usual ex- perimental diet), suggesting the potential existence of an optimum level above which the growth performance is either not affected or decreased. The authors attributed this to a possible disturbance

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids. Moreover, the authors observed a higher total N and ammonium N excretion in the case of the diet supplemented with 20 g/kg benzoic acid. Nevertheless, this was connected to lower ammonia emission from the slurry pit due to the lower pH of the excreta achieved in the case of 20 g/kg benzoic acid.

Zhai et al. (2017) showed a linear increase in average daily weight gain, in FCR and in average daily FI of piglets in the nurs- ery stage when their basal diet (based on maize and soybean meal) was increasingly supplemented with 3 g/kg and 5 g/kg of benzoic acid, during a 28-day trial (average final body weight: 20 kg). Their findings were also similar for pigs in the growing and fattening stages, again for basal diets based on maize and soybean meal and for a 35-day experimental duration in each stage (initial and fi- nal average body weight: 36.1 kg and 112.4 kg). Possible reasons for this improvement in the growth performance of young piglets (nursery stage) which were discussed were the enhancement of the metabolic functions of the gut and the improvement of the ecosystem of microorganisms in the gut, the drop of pH of digesta in ileum, cecum and colon (associated to gut's health improve- ment), the higher activities of enzymes such as trypsin, lipase and amylase in the jejunum (associated to improvement in nutrient di- gestion and absorption) and the improved intestinal morphology, all caused by the addition of benzoic acid. For pigs in the grow- ing stage, an increase in the apparent P and Ca digestibility due to benzoic acid supplementation was discussed as a possible reason for the observed improvement in growth performance. They also suggested an optimal benzoic acid supplementation rate g/kg) for pigs in the growing and fattening stages and the studied pro- duction conditions, implying that growing, finishing pigs may per- form optimally with a lower level of benzoic acid than pigs in the nursery stage. Moreover, the results of Murphy et al. (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. The authors also observed a signifi- cant, linear increase in the ratio N retention to N intake. The total duration of the experimental procedure was 29 days. The reduc- tion in ammonia emission was explained by the decrease in the pH of the stored slurry, the lower availability of the total ammo- niacal nitrogen in the slurry and the increase in the ratio N re- tention to N intake, due to the supplementation of benzoic acid. The authors discussed that benzoic acid reduces urine pH and con- sequently slurry pH due to its conversion to hippuric acid in the liver, which is considered as one of the strongest acids excreted by the kidneys and causes urine acidification. Slurry pH influences the existing ammonia-ammonium cations equilibrium towards ammo- nium cations' production, reducing this way the release of ammo- nia gas from the slurry. They finally discussed that the increase in the ratio N retention to N intake could be caused by a decrease in the total aerobic bacteria number in the ileum, favoring ileal N di- gestibility, by a direct decrease in the pH in the gastric contents or by a reduction in the rate of gastric evacuation. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.
25: Table 6 presents the fattening pigs' performance parameters as a result of their supply with the CNVD and ATTBAD rations. The

pigs' live-weight at sale, the total weight gain (TWG) and the DWG in the fattening period were found significantly larger for the AT- TBAD (p < 0.05). Moreover, the FI and the FCR of the pigs in the fattening period were significantly lower (p < 0.05) when supplied the ATTBAD diet. There was no significant difference in the live- weight of the fatteners at the start of the fattening period when supplied the two diets. These results clearly show the improve- ment on performance parameters such as live body weight and feed efficiency in fattening pigs dietary supplemented with atta- pulgite and benzoic acid for a 59-day treatment.
The biological mechanisms in fattening pigs which explain this positive effect of the combined addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid on body weight gain and FCR remain to be studied.

Regarding the use of attapulgite, its physical properties could be used as a starting point in this respect. Giannenas et al. (2016b) ex- plained that attapulgite is a crystalloid hydrous magnesium- aluminum silicate mineral with a special laminated chain struc- ture in which crystalline lattice displacement exists. The crystals contain varying quantities of Na+, Ca2+, and and have the appearance as needles, fibers, or fibrous clusters. Attapulgite has very good colloidal properties with specific features, includ- ing dispersion. The modified attapulgite has much stronger absorp- tive features (the surface area has increased by 300 times more than common attapulgite), which could promote its functionality (Tang 2014). Kanoulas et al. (2017) used its high-water bind- ing capacity to make the hypothesis that attapulgite induced a de- creased gut transition rate and consequently a gradual release of nutrients in lactating sows, improving this way the mobilization of body reserves for supporting piglet growth up to weaning (associ- ated with a higher body weight loss from late gestation to piglet weaning for the SOWS fed with the standard diet supplemented with attapulgite in comparison to the SOWS fed with the standard diet). In their study, both the standard gestation and lactation di- ets of SOWS in two commercial farms in Greece (based on wheat grain, barley grain, wheat bran and soybean meal) were supple- mented with 7 g/kg of feed of attapulgite and with 8 g/kg of feed of an attapulgite blended product, for two consecutive reproductive phases. In the same study, the SOWS which were fed with the diets supplemented with attapulgite showed improved litter size at 24 postpartum, suggesting a possible positive influence of attapulgite on the embryo survival (improved embryonic and placental devel- opment during the early stages of gestation). They also showed an improved body weight gain of piglets during lactation.

The studies of Lv et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2013) reported an increase in the average daily weight gain and a decrease in the feed to body weight gain ratio of weaned piglets (up to the age of 66 days) supplied (for a 42-day period) with a control diet based on maize and soybean meal which was supplemented with 2 g/kg of diet of attapulgite at the expense of powdered rice hulls, in comparison to the control diet. The authors connected this im- provement in piglets' growth performance with increased trypsin activity in the duodenum and increased DM and GE digestibility. Lv et al. (2015) also reported an increase in CP digestibility and a decrease in the crude fat digestibility for the piglets fed the diet supplemented with attapulgite. The beneficial effect of attapulgite on intestinal integrity of the piglets (Zhang et al., 2013) was re- ported as a possible cause for improvement of the activity of di- gestive enzymes, enhancing DM, GE and CP utilization (Lv et al., 2015). Nevertheless, Lv et al. (2015) further reported negative ef- fects on DM, GE, CP and crude fat digestibility with an increase in attapulgite level from 2 g/kg to 3 g/kg of feed.

Furthermore, Chen et al. (2016) noticed inconsistent growth/laying performance results (positive or no effect) in literature dealing with poultry whose diets were supplemented with attapulgite at levels from 5 to 20 g/kg of diet and for trials' durations from 21 days to 24 weeks. They proposed that the effect

of attapulgite in poultry's growth performance is dependent on the species, the duration of supplementation, the dosage level and the variations in the attapulgite product used. Zhang et al. (2017) also noticed the aforementioned inconsistencies. However, their study indicated a significant increase in the average daily FI and daily weight gain in broilers (in the total 42-day experimental period) as well as a no significant influence to the FCR, when the basal starter and grower diets (based on maize and soybean meal) were supplemented with different levels of attapulgite (from 5 g/kg to 20 g/kg of diet). They explained this growth performance im- provement with the observed quality characteristics' enhancement of the supplied rations (hardness and durability index) after the addition of attapulgite and the increased FI. They also found a potential increase in N retention of broilers between 17-19 days of age and they attributed this to the observed increase in the digesta retention time (possible association with improved N utilization).

DM, CP, lipid and GE retention was also found to be increased in young pigs (12-35 kg of body weight) whose basal diet (based on cereal meals, cereal grains and protein meals of animal and veg- etable origin) was supplemented with 20 g/kg sepiolite, a clay with similar physical properties to attapulgite (Parisini et al., 1999). The authors suggested that for fatteners at this age, sepiolite could fa- vor digestive enzyme activity in the intestine as well as the bind- ing of harmful substances along the gastrointestinal tract. How- ever, no positive effect was observed on the growth performance (body weight gain and FCR) of either growing or fattening pigs (35-100 kg of body weight). Sepiolite has also been proposed to increase digesta retention time, allowing enzymes to provide ex- tended digestion activity of fat, protein and carbohydrates in the gut of pigs and poultry (Ouhida et al., 2000).

Furthermore, dietary inclusion of other clays, such as natural zeolites (e.g. clinoptilolite), have been suggested to promote the growth and nutrient retention in growing pigs (Papaioannou et al., 2002). More specifically, zeolites exert ion exchange, adsorption and catalytic properties, altering the gastrointestinal pH and the ionic composition of the lumen contents, thus affecting the enzy- matic activity of gastrointestinal secretions. Additionally, the well- known affinity of certain zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, for am- monium ions can favor the physiological status of the intesti- nal epithelium and thereby influence the digestive and absorp- tion processes (Papaioannou et al., 2002). It is also worthy of mentioning that natural clinoptilolite and its modified products have been proposed as antioxidants for broiler chicken tissues, enhancing health of broilers (Wu et al., 2013a, 2013b). However, Giannenas et al. (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids. Nevertheless, this was connected to lower ammonia emission from the slurry pit due to the lower pH of the excreta achieved in the case of 20 g/kg benzoic acid.

Zhai et al. (2017) showed a linear increase in average daily weight gain, in FCR and in average daily FI of piglets in the nurs- ery stage when their basal diet (based on maize and soybean meal) was increasingly supplemented with 3 g/kg and 5 g/kg of benzoic acid, during a 28-day trial (average final body weight: 20 kg). Their findings were also similar for pigs in the growing and fattening stages, again for basal diets based on maize and soybean meal and for a 35-day experimental duration in each stage (initial and fi- nal average body weight: 36.1 kg and 112.4 kg). Possible reasons for this improvement in the growth performance of young piglets (nursery stage) which were discussed were the enhancement of the metabolic functions of the gut and the improvement of the ecosystem of microorganisms in the gut, the drop of pH of digesta in ileum, cecum and colon (associated to gut's health improve- ment), the higher activities of enzymes such as trypsin, lipase and amylase in the jejunum (associated to improvement in nutrient di- gestion and absorption) and the improved intestinal morphology, all caused by the addition of benzoic acid. For pigs in the grow- ing stage, an increase in the apparent P and Ca digestibility due to benzoic acid supplementation was discussed as a possible reason for the observed improvement in growth performance. They also suggested an optimal benzoic acid supplementation rate g/kg) for pigs in the growing and fattening stages and the studied pro- duction conditions, implying that growing, finishing pigs may per- form optimally with a lower level of benzoic acid than pigs in the nursery stage. Moreover, the results of Murphy et al. (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. The authors also observed a signifi- cant, linear increase in the ratio N retention to N intake. The total duration of the experimental procedure was 29 days. The reduc- tion in ammonia emission was explained by the decrease in the pH of the stored slurry, the lower availability of the total ammo- niacal nitrogen in the slurry and the increase in the ratio N re- tention to N intake, due to the supplementation of benzoic acid. The authors discussed that benzoic acid reduces urine pH and con- sequently slurry pH due to its conversion to hippuric acid in the liver, which is considered as one of the strongest acids excreted by the kidneys and causes urine acidification. Slurry pH influences the existing ammonia-ammonium cations equilibrium towards ammo- nium cations' production, reducing this way the release of ammo- nia gas from the slurry. They finally discussed that the increase in the ratio N retention to N intake could be caused by a decrease in the total aerobic bacteria number in the ileum, favoring ileal N di- gestibility, by a direct decrease in the pH in the gastric contents or by a reduction in the rate of gastric evacuation. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.
27: Table 6 presents the fattening pigs' performance parameters as a result of their supply with the CNVD and ATTBAD rations. The

pigs' live-weight at sale, the total weight gain (TWG) and the DWG in the fattening period were found significantly larger for the AT- TBAD (p < 0.05). Moreover, the FI and the FCR of the pigs in the fattening period were significantly lower (p < 0.05) when supplied the ATTBAD diet. There was no significant difference in the live- weight of the fatteners at the start of the fattening period when supplied the two diets. These results clearly show the improve- ment on performance parameters such as live body weight and feed efficiency in fattening pigs dietary supplemented with atta- pulgite and benzoic acid for a 59-day treatment.
The biological mechanisms in fattening pigs which explain this positive effect of the combined addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid on body weight gain and FCR remain to be studied.

Regarding the use of attapulgite, its physical properties could be used as a starting point in this respect. Giannenas et al. (2016b) ex- plained that attapulgite is a crystalloid hydrous magnesium- aluminum silicate mineral with a special laminated chain struc- ture in which crystalline lattice displacement exists. The crystals contain varying quantities of Na+, Ca2+, and and have the appearance as needles, fibers, or fibrous clusters. Attapulgite has very good colloidal properties with specific features, includ- ing dispersion. The modified attapulgite has much stronger absorp- tive features (the surface area has increased by 300 times more than common attapulgite), which could promote its functionality (Tang 2014). Kanoulas et al. (2017) used its high-water bind- ing capacity to make the hypothesis that attapulgite induced a de- creased gut transition rate and consequently a gradual release of nutrients in lactating sows, improving this way the mobilization of body reserves for supporting piglet growth up to weaning (associ- ated with a higher body weight loss from late gestation to piglet weaning for the SOWS fed with the standard diet supplemented with attapulgite in comparison to the SOWS fed with the standard diet). In their study, both the standard gestation and lactation di- ets of SOWS in two commercial farms in Greece (based on wheat grain, barley grain, wheat bran and soybean meal) were supple- mented with 7 g/kg of feed of attapulgite and with 8 g/kg of feed of an attapulgite blended product, for two consecutive reproductive phases. In the same study, the SOWS which were fed with the diets supplemented with attapulgite showed improved litter size at 24 postpartum, suggesting a possible positive influence of attapulgite on the embryo survival (improved embryonic and placental devel- opment during the early stages of gestation). They also showed an improved body weight gain of piglets during lactation.

The studies of Lv et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2013) reported an increase in the average daily weight gain and a decrease in the feed to body weight gain ratio of weaned piglets (up to the age of 66 days) supplied (for a 42-day period) with a control diet based on maize and soybean meal which was supplemented with 2 g/kg of diet of attapulgite at the expense of powdered rice hulls, in comparison to the control diet. The authors connected this im- provement in piglets' growth performance with increased trypsin activity in the duodenum and increased DM and GE digestibility. Lv et al. (2015) also reported an increase in CP digestibility and a decrease in the crude fat digestibility for the piglets fed the diet supplemented with attapulgite. The beneficial effect of attapulgite on intestinal integrity of the piglets (Zhang et al., 2013) was re- ported as a possible cause for improvement of the activity of di- gestive enzymes, enhancing DM, GE and CP utilization (Lv et al., 2015). Nevertheless, Lv et al. (2015) further reported negative ef- fects on DM, GE, CP and crude fat digestibility with an increase in attapulgite level from 2 g/kg to 3 g/kg of feed.

Furthermore, Chen et al. (2016) noticed inconsistent growth/laying performance results (positive or no effect) in literature dealing with poultry whose diets were supplemented with attapulgite at levels from 5 to 20 g/kg of diet and for trials' durations from 21 days to 24 weeks. They proposed that the effect

of attapulgite in poultry's growth performance is dependent on the species, the duration of supplementation, the dosage level and the variations in the attapulgite product used. Zhang et al. (2017) also noticed the aforementioned inconsistencies. However, their study indicated a significant increase in the average daily FI and daily weight gain in broilers (in the total 42-day experimental period) as well as a no significant influence to the FCR, when the basal starter and grower diets (based on maize and soybean meal) were supplemented with different levels of attapulgite (from 5 g/kg to 20 g/kg of diet). They explained this growth performance im- provement with the observed quality characteristics' enhancement of the supplied rations (hardness and durability index) after the addition of attapulgite and the increased FI. They also found a potential increase in N retention of broilers between 17-19 days of age and they attributed this to the observed increase in the digesta retention time (possible association with improved N utilization).

DM, CP, lipid and GE retention was also found to be increased in young pigs (12-35 kg of body weight) whose basal diet (based on cereal meals, cereal grains and protein meals of animal and veg- etable origin) was supplemented with 20 g/kg sepiolite, a clay with similar physical properties to attapulgite (Parisini et al., 1999). The authors suggested that for fatteners at this age, sepiolite could fa- vor digestive enzyme activity in the intestine as well as the bind- ing of harmful substances along the gastrointestinal tract. How- ever, no positive effect was observed on the growth performance (body weight gain and FCR) of either growing or fattening pigs (35-100 kg of body weight). Sepiolite has also been proposed to increase digesta retention time, allowing enzymes to provide ex- tended digestion activity of fat, protein and carbohydrates in the gut of pigs and poultry (Ouhida et al., 2000).

Furthermore, dietary inclusion of other clays, such as natural zeolites (e.g. clinoptilolite), have been suggested to promote the growth and nutrient retention in growing pigs (Papaioannou et al., 2002). More specifically, zeolites exert ion exchange, adsorption and catalytic properties, altering the gastrointestinal pH and the ionic composition of the lumen contents, thus affecting the enzy- matic activity of gastrointestinal secretions. Additionally, the well- known affinity of certain zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, for am- monium ions can favor the physiological status of the intesti- nal epithelium and thereby influence the digestive and absorp- tion processes (Papaioannou et al., 2002). It is also worthy of mentioning that natural clinoptilolite and its modified products have been proposed as antioxidants for broiler chicken tissues, enhancing health of broilers (Wu et al., 2013a, 2013b). However, Giannenas et al. (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

Regarding the effect of the addition of benzoic acid to the growth performance of fattening pigs, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) suggested a significant increase in average daily weight gain and daily FI and a significant improvement in FCR for fattening pigs from 43 kg of body weight to their market weight (108.9 kg), when their experimental diet was supplemented with 10 g/kg benzoic acid. Both ex- perimental diets were based on cereal grains, tapioca and soybean meal. However, supplementation with 20 g/kg benzoic acid did not influence the growth performance (in comparison to the usual ex- perimental diet), suggesting the potential existence of an optimum level above which the growth performance is either not affected or decreased. The authors attributed this to a possible disturbance

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids.
