They also suggested an optimal benzoic acid supplementation rate g/kg) for pigs in the growing and fattening stages and the studied pro- duction conditions, implying that growing, finishing pigs may per- form optimally with a lower level of benzoic acid than pigs in the nursery stage. Moreover, the results of Murphy et al. (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. The authors also observed a signifi- cant, linear increase in the ratio N retention to N intake. The total duration of the experimental procedure was 29 days. The reduc- tion in ammonia emission was explained by the decrease in the pH of the stored slurry, the lower availability of the total ammo- niacal nitrogen in the slurry and the increase in the ratio N re- tention to N intake, due to the supplementation of benzoic acid. The authors discussed that benzoic acid reduces urine pH and con- sequently slurry pH due to its conversion to hippuric acid in the liver, which is considered as one of the strongest acids excreted by the kidneys and causes urine acidification. Slurry pH influences the existing ammonia-ammonium cations equilibrium towards ammo- nium cations' production, reducing this way the release of ammo- nia gas from the slurry. They finally discussed that the increase in the ratio N retention to N intake could be caused by a decrease in the total aerobic bacteria number in the ileum, favoring ileal N di- gestibility, by a direct decrease in the pH in the gastric contents or by a reduction in the rate of gastric evacuation. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.
29: Table 6 presents the fattening pigs' performance parameters as a result of their supply with the CNVD and ATTBAD rations. The

pigs' live-weight at sale, the total weight gain (TWG) and the DWG in the fattening period were found significantly larger for the AT- TBAD (p < 0.05). Moreover, the FI and the FCR of the pigs in the fattening period were significantly lower (p < 0.05) when supplied the ATTBAD diet. There was no significant difference in the live- weight of the fatteners at the start of the fattening period when supplied the two diets. These results clearly show the improve- ment on performance parameters such as live body weight and feed efficiency in fattening pigs dietary supplemented with atta- pulgite and benzoic acid for a 59-day treatment.
The biological mechanisms in fattening pigs which explain this positive effect of the combined addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid on body weight gain and FCR remain to be studied.

Regarding the use of attapulgite, its physical properties could be used as a starting point in this respect. Giannenas et al. (2016b) ex- plained that attapulgite is a crystalloid hydrous magnesium- aluminum silicate mineral with a special laminated chain struc- ture in which crystalline lattice displacement exists. The crystals contain varying quantities of Na+, Ca2+, and and have the appearance as needles, fibers, or fibrous clusters. Attapulgite has very good colloidal properties with specific features, includ- ing dispersion. The modified attapulgite has much stronger absorp- tive features (the surface area has increased by 300 times more than common attapulgite), which could promote its functionality (Tang 2014). Kanoulas et al. (2017) used its high-water bind- ing capacity to make the hypothesis that attapulgite induced a de- creased gut transition rate and consequently a gradual release of nutrients in lactating sows, improving this way the mobilization of body reserves for supporting piglet growth up to weaning (associ- ated with a higher body weight loss from late gestation to piglet weaning for the SOWS fed with the standard diet supplemented with attapulgite in comparison to the SOWS fed with the standard diet). In their study, both the standard gestation and lactation di- ets of SOWS in two commercial farms in Greece (based on wheat grain, barley grain, wheat bran and soybean meal) were supple- mented with 7 g/kg of feed of attapulgite and with 8 g/kg of feed of an attapulgite blended product, for two consecutive reproductive phases. In the same study, the SOWS which were fed with the diets supplemented with attapulgite showed improved litter size at 24 postpartum, suggesting a possible positive influence of attapulgite on the embryo survival (improved embryonic and placental devel- opment during the early stages of gestation). They also showed an improved body weight gain of piglets during lactation.

The studies of Lv et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2013) reported an increase in the average daily weight gain and a decrease in the feed to body weight gain ratio of weaned piglets (up to the age of 66 days) supplied (for a 42-day period) with a control diet based on maize and soybean meal which was supplemented with 2 g/kg of diet of attapulgite at the expense of powdered rice hulls, in comparison to the control diet. The authors connected this im- provement in piglets' growth performance with increased trypsin activity in the duodenum and increased DM and GE digestibility. Lv et al. (2015) also reported an increase in CP digestibility and a decrease in the crude fat digestibility for the piglets fed the diet supplemented with attapulgite. The beneficial effect of attapulgite on intestinal integrity of the piglets (Zhang et al., 2013) was re- ported as a possible cause for improvement of the activity of di- gestive enzymes, enhancing DM, GE and CP utilization (Lv et al., 2015). Nevertheless, Lv et al. (2015) further reported negative ef- fects on DM, GE, CP and crude fat digestibility with an increase in attapulgite level from 2 g/kg to 3 g/kg of feed.

Furthermore, Chen et al. (2016) noticed inconsistent growth/laying performance results (positive or no effect) in literature dealing with poultry whose diets were supplemented with attapulgite at levels from 5 to 20 g/kg of diet and for trials' durations from 21 days to 24 weeks. They proposed that the effect

of attapulgite in poultry's growth performance is dependent on the species, the duration of supplementation, the dosage level and the variations in the attapulgite product used. Zhang et al. (2017) also noticed the aforementioned inconsistencies. However, their study indicated a significant increase in the average daily FI and daily weight gain in broilers (in the total 42-day experimental period) as well as a no significant influence to the FCR, when the basal starter and grower diets (based on maize and soybean meal) were supplemented with different levels of attapulgite (from 5 g/kg to 20 g/kg of diet). They explained this growth performance im- provement with the observed quality characteristics' enhancement of the supplied rations (hardness and durability index) after the addition of attapulgite and the increased FI. They also found a potential increase in N retention of broilers between 17-19 days of age and they attributed this to the observed increase in the digesta retention time (possible association with improved N utilization).

DM, CP, lipid and GE retention was also found to be increased in young pigs (12-35 kg of body weight) whose basal diet (based on cereal meals, cereal grains and protein meals of animal and veg- etable origin) was supplemented with 20 g/kg sepiolite, a clay with similar physical properties to attapulgite (Parisini et al., 1999). The authors suggested that for fatteners at this age, sepiolite could fa- vor digestive enzyme activity in the intestine as well as the bind- ing of harmful substances along the gastrointestinal tract. How- ever, no positive effect was observed on the growth performance (body weight gain and FCR) of either growing or fattening pigs (35-100 kg of body weight). Sepiolite has also been proposed to increase digesta retention time, allowing enzymes to provide ex- tended digestion activity of fat, protein and carbohydrates in the gut of pigs and poultry (Ouhida et al., 2000).

Furthermore, dietary inclusion of other clays, such as natural zeolites (e.g. clinoptilolite), have been suggested to promote the growth and nutrient retention in growing pigs (Papaioannou et al., 2002). More specifically, zeolites exert ion exchange, adsorption and catalytic properties, altering the gastrointestinal pH and the ionic composition of the lumen contents, thus affecting the enzy- matic activity of gastrointestinal secretions. Additionally, the well- known affinity of certain zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, for am- monium ions can favor the physiological status of the intesti- nal epithelium and thereby influence the digestive and absorp- tion processes (Papaioannou et al., 2002). It is also worthy of mentioning that natural clinoptilolite and its modified products have been proposed as antioxidants for broiler chicken tissues, enhancing health of broilers (Wu et al., 2013a, 2013b). However, Giannenas et al. (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

Regarding the effect of the addition of benzoic acid to the growth performance of fattening pigs, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) suggested a significant increase in average daily weight gain and daily FI and a significant improvement in FCR for fattening pigs from 43 kg of body weight to their market weight (108.9 kg), when their experimental diet was supplemented with 10 g/kg benzoic acid. In their experiment, supplementation of two different diets (starter and growing/finishing) with 10 g/kg benzoic acid took place for a period of 4 months (one fattening period; starter diet for 4 weeks, growing/finishing diet for the rest of the period), with an average starting weight of 24.3 kg/piglet. Both ex- perimental diets were based on cereal grains, tapioca and soybean meal. However, supplementation with 20 g/kg benzoic acid did not influence the growth performance (in comparison to the usual ex- perimental diet), suggesting the potential existence of an optimum level above which the growth performance is either not affected or decreased. The authors attributed this to a possible disturbance

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids. Moreover, the authors observed a higher total N and ammonium N excretion in the case of the diet supplemented with 20 g/kg benzoic acid. Nevertheless, this was connected to lower ammonia emission from the slurry pit due to the lower pH of the excreta achieved in the case of 20 g/kg benzoic acid.

Zhai et al. (2017) showed a linear increase in average daily weight gain, in FCR and in average daily FI of piglets in the nurs- ery stage when their basal diet (based on maize and soybean meal) was increasingly supplemented with 3 g/kg and 5 g/kg of benzoic acid, during a 28-day trial (average final body weight: 20 kg). (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. The reduc- tion in ammonia emission was explained by the decrease in the pH of the stored slurry, the lower availability of the total ammo- niacal nitrogen in the slurry and the increase in the ratio N re- tention to N intake, due to the supplementation of benzoic acid. The authors discussed that benzoic acid reduces urine pH and con- sequently slurry pH due to its conversion to hippuric acid in the liver, which is considered as one of the strongest acids excreted by the kidneys and causes urine acidification. Slurry pH influences the existing ammonia-ammonium cations equilibrium towards ammo- nium cations' production, reducing this way the release of ammo- nia gas from the slurry. They finally discussed that the increase in the ratio N retention to N intake could be caused by a decrease in the total aerobic bacteria number in the ileum, favoring ileal N di- gestibility, by a direct decrease in the pH in the gastric contents or by a reduction in the rate of gastric evacuation. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.
31: Table 6 presents the fattening pigs' performance parameters as a result of their supply with the CNVD and ATTBAD rations. The

pigs' live-weight at sale, the total weight gain (TWG) and the DWG in the fattening period were found significantly larger for the AT- TBAD (p < 0.05). Moreover, the FI and the FCR of the pigs in the fattening period were significantly lower (p < 0.05) when supplied the ATTBAD diet. There was no significant difference in the live- weight of the fatteners at the start of the fattening period when supplied the two diets. These results clearly show the improve- ment on performance parameters such as live body weight and feed efficiency in fattening pigs dietary supplemented with atta- pulgite and benzoic acid for a 59-day treatment.
The biological mechanisms in fattening pigs which explain this positive effect of the combined addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid on body weight gain and FCR remain to be studied.

Regarding the use of attapulgite, its physical properties could be used as a starting point in this respect. Giannenas et al. (2016b) ex- plained that attapulgite is a crystalloid hydrous magnesium- aluminum silicate mineral with a special laminated chain struc- ture in which crystalline lattice displacement exists. The crystals contain varying quantities of Na+, Ca2+, and and have the appearance as needles, fibers, or fibrous clusters. Attapulgite has very good colloidal properties with specific features, includ- ing dispersion. The modified attapulgite has much stronger absorp- tive features (the surface area has increased by 300 times more than common attapulgite), which could promote its functionality (Tang 2014). Kanoulas et al. (2017) used its high-water bind- ing capacity to make the hypothesis that attapulgite induced a de- creased gut transition rate and consequently a gradual release of nutrients in lactating sows, improving this way the mobilization of body reserves for supporting piglet growth up to weaning (associ- ated with a higher body weight loss from late gestation to piglet weaning for the SOWS fed with the standard diet supplemented with attapulgite in comparison to the SOWS fed with the standard diet). In their study, both the standard gestation and lactation di- ets of SOWS in two commercial farms in Greece (based on wheat grain, barley grain, wheat bran and soybean meal) were supple- mented with 7 g/kg of feed of attapulgite and with 8 g/kg of feed of an attapulgite blended product, for two consecutive reproductive phases. In the same study, the SOWS which were fed with the diets supplemented with attapulgite showed improved litter size at 24 postpartum, suggesting a possible positive influence of attapulgite on the embryo survival (improved embryonic and placental devel- opment during the early stages of gestation). They also showed an improved body weight gain of piglets during lactation.

The studies of Lv et al. (2015) and Zhang et al. (2013) reported an increase in the average daily weight gain and a decrease in the feed to body weight gain ratio of weaned piglets (up to the age of 66 days) supplied (for a 42-day period) with a control diet based on maize and soybean meal which was supplemented with 2 g/kg of diet of attapulgite at the expense of powdered rice hulls, in comparison to the control diet. The authors connected this im- provement in piglets' growth performance with increased trypsin activity in the duodenum and increased DM and GE digestibility. Lv et al. (2015) also reported an increase in CP digestibility and a decrease in the crude fat digestibility for the piglets fed the diet supplemented with attapulgite. The beneficial effect of attapulgite on intestinal integrity of the piglets (Zhang et al., 2013) was re- ported as a possible cause for improvement of the activity of di- gestive enzymes, enhancing DM, GE and CP utilization (Lv et al., 2015). Nevertheless, Lv et al. (2015) further reported negative ef- fects on DM, GE, CP and crude fat digestibility with an increase in attapulgite level from 2 g/kg to 3 g/kg of feed.

Furthermore, Chen et al. (2016) noticed inconsistent growth/laying performance results (positive or no effect) in literature dealing with poultry whose diets were supplemented with attapulgite at levels from 5 to 20 g/kg of diet and for trials' durations from 21 days to 24 weeks. They proposed that the effect

of attapulgite in poultry's growth performance is dependent on the species, the duration of supplementation, the dosage level and the variations in the attapulgite product used. Zhang et al. (2017) also noticed the aforementioned inconsistencies. However, their study indicated a significant increase in the average daily FI and daily weight gain in broilers (in the total 42-day experimental period) as well as a no significant influence to the FCR, when the basal starter and grower diets (based on maize and soybean meal) were supplemented with different levels of attapulgite (from 5 g/kg to 20 g/kg of diet). They explained this growth performance im- provement with the observed quality characteristics' enhancement of the supplied rations (hardness and durability index) after the addition of attapulgite and the increased FI. They also found a potential increase in N retention of broilers between 17-19 days of age and they attributed this to the observed increase in the digesta retention time (possible association with improved N utilization).

DM, CP, lipid and GE retention was also found to be increased in young pigs (12-35 kg of body weight) whose basal diet (based on cereal meals, cereal grains and protein meals of animal and veg- etable origin) was supplemented with 20 g/kg sepiolite, a clay with similar physical properties to attapulgite (Parisini et al., 1999). The authors suggested that for fatteners at this age, sepiolite could fa- vor digestive enzyme activity in the intestine as well as the bind- ing of harmful substances along the gastrointestinal tract. How- ever, no positive effect was observed on the growth performance (body weight gain and FCR) of either growing or fattening pigs (35-100 kg of body weight). Sepiolite has also been proposed to increase digesta retention time, allowing enzymes to provide ex- tended digestion activity of fat, protein and carbohydrates in the gut of pigs and poultry (Ouhida et al., 2000).

Furthermore, dietary inclusion of other clays, such as natural zeolites (e.g. clinoptilolite), have been suggested to promote the growth and nutrient retention in growing pigs (Papaioannou et al., 2002). More specifically, zeolites exert ion exchange, adsorption and catalytic properties, altering the gastrointestinal pH and the ionic composition of the lumen contents, thus affecting the enzy- matic activity of gastrointestinal secretions. Additionally, the well- known affinity of certain zeolites, such as clinoptilolite, for am- monium ions can favor the physiological status of the intesti- nal epithelium and thereby influence the digestive and absorp- tion processes (Papaioannou et al., 2002). It is also worthy of mentioning that natural clinoptilolite and its modified products have been proposed as antioxidants for broiler chicken tissues, enhancing health of broilers (Wu et al., 2013a, 2013b). However, Giannenas et al. (2016b) also suggested a potential improvement of antioxidant properties of the commercial feeds (based on maize and soybean meal) of broiler chickens further supplemented with an oregano essential oil blend (0.3 g/kg of feed) and attapulgite (3 g/kg of feed), after a 42-day trial.

Regarding the effect of the addition of benzoic acid to the growth performance of fattening pigs, Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) suggested a significant increase in average daily weight gain and daily FI and a significant improvement in FCR for fattening pigs from 43 kg of body weight to their market weight (108.9 kg), when their experimental diet was supplemented with 10 g/kg benzoic acid. In their experiment, supplementation of two different diets (starter and growing/finishing) with 10 g/kg benzoic acid took place for a period of 4 months (one fattening period; starter diet for 4 weeks, growing/finishing diet for the rest of the period), with an average starting weight of 24.3 kg/piglet. Both ex- perimental diets were based on cereal grains, tapioca and soybean meal. However, supplementation with 20 g/kg benzoic acid did not influence the growth performance (in comparison to the usual ex- perimental diet), suggesting the potential existence of an optimum level above which the growth performance is either not affected or decreased. The authors attributed this to a possible disturbance

in the acid-base balance of the pigs. However, the enhancement of the growth performance associated with the addition of 10 g/kg benzoic acid was attributed to a better digestibility of amino acids. Moreover, the authors observed a higher total N and ammonium N excretion in the case of the diet supplemented with 20 g/kg benzoic acid. Nevertheless, this was connected to lower ammonia emission from the slurry pit due to the lower pH of the excreta achieved in the case of 20 g/kg benzoic acid.

Zhai et al. (2017) showed a linear increase in average daily weight gain, in FCR and in average daily FI of piglets in the nurs- ery stage when their basal diet (based on maize and soybean meal) was increasingly supplemented with 3 g/kg and 5 g/kg of benzoic acid, during a 28-day trial (average final body weight: 20 kg). Their findings were also similar for pigs in the growing and fattening stages, again for basal diets based on maize and soybean meal and for a 35-day experimental duration in each stage (initial and fi- nal average body weight: 36.1 kg and 112.4 kg). Possible reasons for this improvement in the growth performance of young piglets (nursery stage) which were discussed were the enhancement of the metabolic functions of the gut and the improvement of the ecosystem of microorganisms in the gut, the drop of pH of digesta in ileum, cecum and colon (associated to gut's health improve- ment), the higher activities of enzymes such as trypsin, lipase and amylase in the jejunum (associated to improvement in nutrient di- gestion and absorption) and the improved intestinal morphology, all caused by the addition of benzoic acid. For pigs in the grow- ing stage, an increase in the apparent P and Ca digestibility due to benzoic acid supplementation was discussed as a possible reason for the observed improvement in growth performance. They also suggested an optimal benzoic acid supplementation rate g/kg) for pigs in the growing and fattening stages and the studied pro- duction conditions, implying that growing, finishing pigs may per- form optimally with a lower level of benzoic acid than pigs in the nursery stage. Moreover, the results of Murphy et al. (2011) showed a signifi- cant, linear reduction in ammonia emission from manure excreted from fattening pigs (average initial body weight: 64 kg) during storage, in the total ammoniacal nitrogen and total Kjeldahl nitro- gen concentrations in the stored manure, in the urinary pH and in the slurry pH during storage, as the level of supplementation of benzoic acid to an experimental diet (based on cereal grains and soybean meal) increased from 0 g/kg to 10, 20 and 30 g/kg, at the expense of wheat grain. It is obvious that none of the aforementioned literature find- ings could be used to directly decipher the mechanisms behind the beneficial combined supplementation of attapulgite and ben- zoic acid in the current experimental procedure. There are varia- tions between the current experimental procedure and the ones already described, starting from the fact that different pig cohorts

(e.g. SOWS (Kanoulas et al. 2017)) or ages of fatteners (piglets in the nursery stage (Zhang et al., 2013; Lv et al., 2015) were concerned when studying dietary attapulgite supplementation, or even differ- ent livestock species (poultry, e.g. Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). Regarding benzoic acid supplementation, studies for fatten- ing pigs were found, which however dealt with different durations of supplementation and different basal diets. The level of benzoic acid in the current experiment (5 g/kg) is higher than the opti- mal level (3.6 proposed by Zhai et al. (2017) for the growth performance of growing / fattening pigs and a similar basal diet. Moreover, the increase in the DWG and the decrease in the FCR were found to be larger in the current study (11.2% and 15.5%, re- spectively) than in the study of Zhai et al. (2017) (6% and 3.6%, respectively). This could be an indication that attapulgite and ben- zoic acid at these levels have a more beneficial effect on the DWG and FCR than only benzoic acid for fattening pigs. Improvement of intestinal functionality (e.g. of digestive enzymes' activity), an in- crease in digesta retention time and a drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract seem to be possible reasons for the observed improvement of the growth performance of the fattening pigs sup- plied the ATTBAD diet. The existing literature suggests a possible increase in N retention due to the supplementation of both at- tapulgite and benzoic acid in the diet of fattening pigs. However, there is no scientific evidence that the increased growth of fatten- ing pigs is associated with increased fat digestibility. Moreover, lit- erature has confirmed the ammonia mitigation potential of supple- menting fattening pigs' diets with benzoic acid. However, there is no such evidence for the combined dietary addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid.

Van der Peet-Schwering et al. (1999) showed that the gross margin per fattener at the finishing stage is a function of the yield (due to the sale of pig live-weight), the costs for feed purchase and other costs (e.g. health costs, costs of disposal etc.). In our study, the supply of the ATTBAD diet was shown to be connected with a significant increase in the final live-weight of fattening pigs and a significant decrease in the total FI in the fattening stage. This implies an increase in the yield per fattening pig and a decrease in the quantities purchased for all feed ingredients in the fatten- ing stage except attapulgite and benzoic acid. Assuming a non- significant difference in the other costs when supplying the AT- TBAD diet in comparison to the CNVD diet, the difference between the yield and the feed costs will actually determine the gross mar- gin per fattener in the pig farm. Due to the increased price of at- tapulgite and benzoic acid products in comparison to maize grain, a potential increase in the purchase price per kg of feed for pigs in the fattening stage should be expected. However, due to the de- crease in the FI, the size of the difference between the purchase price of the CNVD and ATTBAD diets will determine if there is a profit from the feed purchase connected to this transition and eventually the gross margin of the farm per fattening pig. Although it was out of the scope of this work to study such economic ef- fects, an average increase in the feed cost per kg not more than 3% should be expected, which will eventually result in a potential increase in the gross margin per finished pig in the farm in case of supplying the ATTBAD diet.
33: Environmental performance. Table 7 presents the potential es- timates per FU for the environmental impact category indicators (EICIs) assessed. All the EICI estimates were larger for the system using the CNVD for the fattening pigs. This means that the ATTBAD system seems to be associated with an improved environmental performance as compared to the CNVD system.
The ATTBAD system was connected to lower annual inputs of materials and electricity (apart from attapulgite and benzoic acid)

and lower annual emission outputs per FU (Table 5). Nevertheless, the annual inputs of attapulgite and benzoic acid and their envi- ronmental impact per kg of supply were low (Tab. D1, Supplemen- tary Material) to negatively affect the environmental performance of the ATTBAD system in comparison to the CNVD system. Thus, the results of Table 4 can be explained.

The annual size of material inputs to the pig farm is a di- rect consequence of the FI in the fattening stage. The decreased FI (Table 6) in the ATTBAD system leads to decreased annual inputs of dried maize and barley grains, wheat bran, soybean meal and oil and mineral feed for fatteners. Moreover, the increased TWG of pigs when supplied the ATTBAD diet (Table 6) was connected to increased final live-weight per finisher and therefore to increased annual pig live-weight yield. This explains why even the inputs whose annual size was not influenced (i.e. herring meal, milk re- placer and sweet whey) or not considered to be influenced (i.e. water and electricity supply) by the diet modification, showed a lower value per FU in the ATTBAD system.

The lower estimates of CH4 from pigs' enteric fermentation and of PM emissions per FU in the ATTBAD system (Table 5) are also exclusively explained by the higher annual live-weight yield, as the Tier 1 approaches used for their calculation (EEA, 2016; IPCC, 2006a) employ constant EFs per head.

For the rest of the emissions, other parameters influencing their size per FU (apart from the annual live-weight yield), are the DE, the DM, the ash and the CP contents of the CNVD and ATTBAD. However, the difference in these parameters between the two di- ets is low (Table 1), implying small influence on the difference between the emissions' estimates. Concerning CH4 emission from OFSM, the total excretion of VS per fattening pig was highly in- fluenced by the total ration's intake (influence on total digestible energy, GE, urinary energy and Dry Matter Intake (DMI) per fat- tening pig - all larger in the CNVD system). Furthermore, larger ration consumption in the CNVD system was connected to larger total ration losses per fattening pig, influencing the quantity of VS in feed waste (larger in the CNVD system).

With regards to N emissions, the higher N intake per fatten- ing pig in the CNVD system was only dependent on their rations' intake. Moreover, the higher N retention per fattening pig in the ATTBAD system could be connected to its higher slaughter live- weight (ASAE, 2005). As an outcome, total N excretion was higher
in the CNVD system, leading to higher NH3, N20 and NO2 emis- sions from OFSM.
Finally, the estimation method of NMVOC emissions (EEA, 2016) indicated that the higher estimates in the CNVD system could be also explained by the total FI per fattening pig.

Contribution analysis and hot spots. Fig. 2 illustrates the % contribution of the supply of the various rations' ingredients and electricity as well as the pig farm to the EICIs assessed for the AT- TBAD system (Table 7).

It should be noticed that CNVD and ATTBAD systems presented similar behavior with respect to the way the various contributors influenced the EICI estimates. The % contribution differences be- tween the two systems were lower than 6% for all EICIs except for the indicator for fossil fuel depletion (ARDF) (12.5% higher contri- bution for the ATTBAD system). The % contribution of attapulgite and benzoic acid supplies was very low for all EICIs (<2.1%). The maximal contribution of benzoic acid supply was to ARDF (2.07%), explaining the aforementioned contribution difference for this EICI. The pig farm was the most important contributor to the CC (GWP- 100) mostly due to CH4 emission from the OFSM system (27.74% of the total estimate), acidification (AE-A (and AP)) and terrestrial eutrophication (AE-TE), exclusively due to the direct NH3 emis- sions from the farm buildings (PH) and the slurry storage facil- ity. In Fig. 2, the pig farm sub-system includes all the green- house gas (GHG) and pollutant emissions to air, estimated at the pig farm level (Table 5). Furthermore, maize grain supply was the most relevant for the marine eutrophication (FNMC), eutrophica- tion (EP) (mostly due to direct NO3 emissions to water bodies from the application of N fertilizers in domestic maize cultivation) and ARDF (mostly due to the electricity use in drying and irriga- tion in domestic maize production) while the barley grain supply for the freshwater eutrophication (FPFC), agricultural land occupa- tion (LOP) and water use (UDP). Finally, soybean meal supply was the most important contributor to the CC due to dLUC (GWP-100 (dLUC)).

Comparison with literature.C limate change. A range of esti- mates from 2.25 to 3.42 kg CO2-eq per kg pig live-weight at the farm gate was reported in relevant studies (González-García et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017). Although it is not clearly stated, the GHG emissions due to dLUC associated to feed production seem to have not been included in these values. Suggested contributions of the emissions from dLUC for soybean production in Brazil were 0.32 kg CO2-eq per kg pig at the slaughterhouse gate for a similar pro- duction system in northern Germany (Reckmann et al., 2016) and 1.0 kg CO2-eq per kg pig live-weight at the farm gate for the heavy pig production system in Italy (Bava et al., 2017).

The estimates for the GWP-100 in this study (Table 7) were higher than the values suggested in the aforementioned range. It seems that CH4 emission from the OFSM per kg pig live-weight in this study (most important contributor to GWP-100) was higher than the respective estimates in this literature. In Greece, due to higher average annual temperature than in countries of west- ern Europe, an increased methane conversion factor is expected (IPCC, 2006a). Furthermore, in accordance with FAO (2018), the VS from the feed waste were also included in the estimation of CH4, increasing its annual estimate, while in the relevant literature studies this was not included in the calculations. The characteri- zation factor for biogenic CH4 in the IPCC 2013 method has been increased (27.75 kg CO2-eq/kg) in comparison to the impact as- sessment methods of previous studies (e.g. ReCiPe 2008, EDIP and CML 2000 baseline). Another important factor is that the Greek electricity mix was much dependent on the domestic electricity production from lignite (Ecoinvent v.3.4 in (Pré, 2017)), an impor- tant fossil CO2 emitting process, while this is not the case for the countries of Western Europe (e.g. the electricity mix in Spain is basically composed of electricity produced in nuclear, hydroelec-

Regarding the GWP-100 (dLUC) estimate, the values in this study (Table 7) were close to the value reported in Bava et al. (2017). In this work, the GWP-100 (dLUC) was mostly connected to CO2 emissions due to the dLUC for soybean produc- tion in Argentina and Brazil, Both the estimation of this emis- sion per ha of land transformed and the allocation of the land transformation to soybean in Argentina and Brazil were per- formed following the Ecoinvent v.3.4 methodology (Pré, 2017). Reckmann et al. (2016) used a value of 2081 kg CO2-eq/ha, re- sulting from a previous version of the Ecoinvent methodology (re- garding the assumptions for the type of land modified in favor of soybean production) and by considering dLUC only for soybean production and that soybean was exclusively produced in Brazil. Bava et al. (2017) used a previous version of the Agri-footprint database (similar method to this study), associated with an esti- mate of 10790 kg CO2-eq/ha of soybean in Brazil (again only con- sidering dLUC in Brazil). In this work, the assumed land transfor- mations were connected to estimates of 10328.6 kg CO2-eq/ha and 13572 kg CO2-eq/ha for soybean cultivation in Brazil and Argentina, respectively. The almost equivalent contribution of LUC from soy- bean cultivation in Brazil and Argentina to GWP-100 (dLUC), the differences between the aforementioned values per ha of soybean and the annual soybean-based inputs to the pig farming systems of these studies, could explain the increased values of the total GWP- 100 (dLUC) in this work.

Acidification. Most of the studies presented results in the range 35-56.5 g SO2-eq/kg pig live-weight (González-García et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017). Our estimates for AP (Table 7) were in agree- ment with these values, although closer to the upper end of the range. The high contribution of on-farm NH3 emission to the AP estimate in our study indicates that differences in the OFSM sys- tems between the studied system and the systems in countries of Western Europe (e.g. in the manure storage facilities considered:
Other feed ingredients

longer in-house storage of slurry in a pit beneath the slatted floor of the pig house in the study of González-García et al. (2015) VS. daily pumping of the pig excreta to an open earthen pond in this study) as well as in the estimation of the N flows (in N reten- tion percentages and in NH3-N EFs from housing and outside ma- nure storage facilities) are mostly responsible for the increased es- timates in this study.

Eutrophication. For FPFC, lower values were found in the liter- ature (0.20 g P eq/kg pig live-weight, Noya et al (2017); 0.435 g P eq/kg pig live-weight, González-García et al. (2015)) than in this study (Table 7). FPFC in this work was basically connected to the P emissions to surface water from the cultivation of domes- tic cereal grains. The Increased P emissions to surface water per kg pig live-weight could therefore be the reason for the higher FPFC estimate of this work. These emission flows were modeled for the background system following the methodology presented in Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011), in our effort to adjust the existing Ecoivent V. 3.4 cereal grain datasets to Greek production conditions (Table C1, Supplementary Material). According to this methodol- ogy, one of the emission flows to water estimated is a total P flow to surface water as a function of the annual quantity of the water-eroded soil per ha of cultivated land. The observed differ- ence could therefore come as a consequence of the potential extent of water erosion of cultivated soils in Greece (Dimoyiannis et al., 2006). FNMC (Table 7) was found between the estimates of González-García et al. (2015) (32.19 g N eq/kg live-weight) and Noya et al (2017) (50.1 g N eq/kg live-weight). Concerning the global eutrophication impact (EP), this work was in line with the range of estimates reported in Noya et al (2017) (19.0-45.7 g PO4³ eq/kg live-weight).

Land use. Noya et al (2017) reported a range of 4.40 to 6.55 n2yr per kg pig live-weight at the farm gate. The results of this study (Table 7) were in accordance with this range. The rela- tively large values in the current study could be explained by the fact that the ReCiPe 2016 method (which was used in the current study) also considered CF's for LUC in the estimation of LOP while
the ReCiPe 2008 method in accordance to which the literature re- sults were corrected in the work of Noya et al. (2017) did not, im- plying an additional contribution of LUC in the LOP in comparison

to the Fossil relevant fuel depletion. literature. A range of estimates from 12.5 to|to the Fossil relevant fuel depletion. literature. A range of estimates from 12.5 to||| 18.1 MJ/kg live-weight has been suggested (Noya et al., 2017). Our results for ARDF were higher than those estimates. Nevertheless, they are in line (Fig. 2) with the literature findings that more than 80% of the fossil fuel depletion can be attributed to the feed sup- ply to the pig farm (e.g. González-García et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017; van Zanten et al., 2015). Water use. Use of the ReCiPe 2008 method, would allow us to receive 1.3 m3/kg live-weight and 1.19 n³/kg live-weight of water depletion (WD) for the CNVD and the ATTBAD systems, respectively. These estimates are lower than the respective es- timate which can be calculated from the work of González-|||| García et al. (2015) (i.e. 2.28 m³ /kg live-weight). Nevertheless, our findings agree with the latter study regarding the contribution of the pig farming system (pig farm + electricity supply) and the total feed supply to the total WD potential.|||| In this study, manure application to agricultural land was not considered in the system boundaries, in line with FAO (2018) for the case of treating manure as a residual. Thus, potentially avoided burdens for CC, acidification and eutrophication from mineral fer- tilizer production and land application were not considered. This could possibly be another explanation for the higher estimates in the current study for these EICIs. Finally, considering the impor- tance of feed ingredients' supply chains to all EICs (Fig. 2), the increased quantity of feed input per kg of pig live-weight in the systems of this study (CNVD: 3.48 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight; ATTBAD: 3.21 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight) in comparison to other studies (e.g. 2.46 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight in Noya et al (2017); 2.53 kg of feed/kg pig LW in Reckmann et al. (2013)) could explain the relatively increased EICI estimates in this work. Life Cycle Inventory data quality and uncertainty assess- ment. Data quality is discussed in the context of the pig farm- specific datasets developed for the CNVD and ATTBAD systems. The data quality indicators and levels were in agreement with EC-JRC (2010) and assigned in line with European Commis- sion (2018) in order to ensure consistency. The contribution analysis suggests that the pig farm-specific datasets are from a data quality point of view strongly affected by the secondary datasets (SeDs) associated with maize grain, barley grain, wheat bran, soybean meal and electricity supply as well as the activity data (AD) which determine their annual input quanti- ties to the pig farm. Furthermore, annual CH4, N20 and NH3 emis- sions (elementary flows, (EIFIs)) from the pig farm seem to be of importance. Given the fact that good time representativeness was ensured for the AD and the on-farm EIFIs and that SeDs were se- lected to this purpose, technological and geographical representa- tiveness as well as precision were the quality indicators of interest. Tables 8-10 respectively present data quality information for the AD, SeDs and EIFIs connected to the hot spots of the studied systems. The modifications applied in the original SeDs (Tables C1 and C2, Supplementary material - all processes except medium voltage electricity grid mix) generally rendered the resulting datasets more country-specific, improving the GeR level for the datasets used in the LCA models (in comparison to the level shown in Table 9). Tables 8-10 suggest a fair data quality level for the data quality indicators of the majority of AD, SeDs and EIFIs, and therefore fair data quality for the total LCI of both systems.||||

Nevertheless, data quality has a great potential for improve- ment. This could be achieved by developing country-specific LCI datasets for domestic cereal grains' cultivation and drying pro- cesses, cereal grain milling and oilseed crushing industrial pro-

cesses but also domestic market datasets which involve imports of feed components, in accordance with internationally accepted data quality guidelines (e.g. Fazio et al., 2019).

Monte Carlo analysis was finally performed as an approach to incorporate the uncertainty which is caused by the AD, SeD and farm level ElFls to the EICI results. In this purpose, the relevant SimaPro function was employed and a comparison between the CNVD and ATTBAD systems' EICI estimates was configured. The Monte Carlo stop criterion was set equal to 500 runs. Lognor- mal distributions were assumed for zootechnical parameters (apart from the performance parameters for fattening pigs in Table 6, for which a normal distribution was considered), for the nutri- ents of the various pig diets and for the manure properties. Pedi- gree matrices (e.g. Goedkoop et al., 2016) were completed for all these parameters in order to obtain geometrical standard devia- tions for their distributions. Normal, lognormal and triangle dis- tributions were considered for the emission estimation parameters (e.g. N retention, EFs etc.) which were taken from IPCC (2006a) and EEA (2016), exploiting the uncertainty information (e.g. ranges of estimates) provided in these documents. Pedigree matrices were finally modified for all the Ecoinvent V. 3.4. SeDs which were modified in this study and completed for the Greek market mix
datasets. The uncertainty analysis results are shown in Table 11 and refer to a 95% confidence interval.

Table 11 suggests significantly lower values for all the EICIs of the ATTBAD system in comparison to the CNVD system, with 95% confidence. It has to be noticed that not all sources and types of uncertainty in an ELCA (e.g. Finnveden et al., 2009) can be ad- dressed with such a Monte Carlo probabilistic simulation. However, these results could be used to further support the finding that the supply of the ATTBAD diet to fattening pigs could improve the en- vironmental performance of the studied system in comparison to the supply of the CNVD diet (Table 7).

The pig farm was the most important contributor to the CC (GWP- 100) mostly due to CH4 emission from the OFSM system (27.74% of the total estimate), acidification (AE-A (and AP)) and terrestrial eutrophication (AE-TE), exclusively due to the direct NH3 emis- sions from the farm buildings (PH) and the slurry storage facil- ity. In Fig. 2, the pig farm sub-system includes all the green- house gas (GHG) and pollutant emissions to air, estimated at the pig farm level (Table 5). Furthermore, maize grain supply was the most relevant for the marine eutrophication (FNMC), eutrophica- tion (EP) (mostly due to direct NO3 emissions to water bodies from the application of N fertilizers in domestic maize cultivation) and ARDF (mostly due to the electricity use in drying and irriga- tion in domestic maize production) while the barley grain supply for the freshwater eutrophication (FPFC), agricultural land occupa- tion (LOP) and water use (UDP). Finally, soybean meal supply was the most important contributor to the CC due to dLUC (GWP-100 (dLUC)).

Comparison with literature.C limate change. A range of esti- mates from 2.25 to 3.42 kg CO2-eq per kg pig live-weight at the farm gate was reported in relevant studies (González-García et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017). Although it is not clearly stated, the GHG emissions due to dLUC associated to feed production seem to have not been included in these values. Suggested contributions of the emissions from dLUC for soybean production in Brazil were 0.32 kg CO2-eq per kg pig at the slaughterhouse gate for a similar pro- duction system in northern Germany (Reckmann et al., 2016) and 1.0 kg CO2-eq per kg pig live-weight at the farm gate for the heavy pig production system in Italy (Bava et al., 2017).

The estimates for the GWP-100 in this study (Table 7) were higher than the values suggested in the aforementioned range. It seems that CH4 emission from the OFSM per kg pig live-weight in this study (most important contributor to GWP-100) was higher than the respective estimates in this literature. In Greece, due to higher average annual temperature than in countries of west- ern Europe, an increased methane conversion factor is expected (IPCC, 2006a). Furthermore, in accordance with FAO (2018), the VS from the feed waste were also included in the estimation of CH4, increasing its annual estimate, while in the relevant literature studies this was not included in the calculations. The characteri- zation factor for biogenic CH4 in the IPCC 2013 method has been increased (27.75 kg CO2-eq/kg) in comparison to the impact as- sessment methods of previous studies (e.g. ReCiPe 2008, EDIP and CML 2000 baseline). Another important factor is that the Greek electricity mix was much dependent on the domestic electricity production from lignite (Ecoinvent v.3.4 in (Pré, 2017)), an impor- tant fossil CO2 emitting process, while this is not the case for the countries of Western Europe (e.g. the electricity mix in Spain is basically composed of electricity produced in nuclear, hydroelec-

Regarding the GWP-100 (dLUC) estimate, the values in this study (Table 7) were close to the value reported in Bava et al. (2017). In this work, the GWP-100 (dLUC) was mostly connected to CO2 emissions due to the dLUC for soybean produc- tion in Argentina and Brazil, Both the estimation of this emis- sion per ha of land transformed and the allocation of the land transformation to soybean in Argentina and Brazil were per- formed following the Ecoinvent v.3.4 methodology (Pré, 2017). Reckmann et al. (2016) used a value of 2081 kg CO2-eq/ha, re- sulting from a previous version of the Ecoinvent methodology (re- garding the assumptions for the type of land modified in favor of soybean production) and by considering dLUC only for soybean production and that soybean was exclusively produced in Brazil. Bava et al. (2017) used a previous version of the Agri-footprint database (similar method to this study), associated with an esti- mate of 10790 kg CO2-eq/ha of soybean in Brazil (again only con- sidering dLUC in Brazil). In this work, the assumed land transfor- mations were connected to estimates of 10328.6 kg CO2-eq/ha and 13572 kg CO2-eq/ha for soybean cultivation in Brazil and Argentina, respectively. The almost equivalent contribution of LUC from soy- bean cultivation in Brazil and Argentina to GWP-100 (dLUC), the differences between the aforementioned values per ha of soybean and the annual soybean-based inputs to the pig farming systems of these studies, could explain the increased values of the total GWP- 100 (dLUC) in this work.

Acidification. Most of the studies presented results in the range 35-56.5 g SO2-eq/kg pig live-weight (González-García et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017). Our estimates for AP (Table 7) were in agree- ment with these values, although closer to the upper end of the range. The high contribution of on-farm NH3 emission to the AP estimate in our study indicates that differences in the OFSM sys- tems between the studied system and the systems in countries of Western Europe (e.g. in the manure storage facilities considered:
Other feed ingredients

longer in-house storage of slurry in a pit beneath the slatted floor of the pig house in the study of González-García et al. (2015) VS. daily pumping of the pig excreta to an open earthen pond in this study) as well as in the estimation of the N flows (in N reten- tion percentages and in NH3-N EFs from housing and outside ma- nure storage facilities) are mostly responsible for the increased es- timates in this study.

Eutrophication. For FPFC, lower values were found in the liter- ature (0.20 g P eq/kg pig live-weight, Noya et al (2017); 0.435 g P eq/kg pig live-weight, González-García et al. (2015)) than in this study (Table 7). FPFC in this work was basically connected to the P emissions to surface water from the cultivation of domes- tic cereal grains. The Increased P emissions to surface water per kg pig live-weight could therefore be the reason for the higher FPFC estimate of this work. These emission flows were modeled for the background system following the methodology presented in Nemecek and Schnetzer (2011), in our effort to adjust the existing Ecoivent V. 3.4 cereal grain datasets to Greek production conditions (Table C1, Supplementary Material). According to this methodol- ogy, one of the emission flows to water estimated is a total P flow to surface water as a function of the annual quantity of the water-eroded soil per ha of cultivated land. The observed differ- ence could therefore come as a consequence of the potential extent of water erosion of cultivated soils in Greece (Dimoyiannis et al., 2006). FNMC (Table 7) was found between the estimates of González-García et al. (2015) (32.19 g N eq/kg live-weight) and Noya et al (2017) (50.1 g N eq/kg live-weight). Concerning the global eutrophication impact (EP), this work was in line with the range of estimates reported in Noya et al (2017) (19.0-45.7 g PO4³ eq/kg live-weight).

Land use. Noya et al (2017) reported a range of 4.40 to 6.55 n2yr per kg pig live-weight at the farm gate. The results of this study (Table 7) were in accordance with this range. The rela- tively large values in the current study could be explained by the fact that the ReCiPe 2016 method (which was used in the current study) also considered CF's for LUC in the estimation of LOP while
the ReCiPe 2008 method in accordance to which the literature re- sults were corrected in the work of Noya et al. (2017) did not, im- plying an additional contribution of LUC in the LOP in comparison

to the Fossil relevant fuel depletion. literature. A range of estimates from 12.5 to|to the Fossil relevant fuel depletion. literature. A range of estimates from 12.5 to||| 18.1 MJ/kg live-weight has been suggested (Noya et al., 2017). Our results for ARDF were higher than those estimates. Nevertheless, they are in line (Fig. 2) with the literature findings that more than 80% of the fossil fuel depletion can be attributed to the feed sup- ply to the pig farm (e.g. González-García et al., 2015; Noya et al., 2017; van Zanten et al., 2015). Water use. Use of the ReCiPe 2008 method, would allow us to receive 1.3 m3/kg live-weight and 1.19 n³/kg live-weight of water depletion (WD) for the CNVD and the ATTBAD systems, respectively. These estimates are lower than the respective es- timate which can be calculated from the work of González-|||| García et al. (2015) (i.e. 2.28 m³ /kg live-weight). Nevertheless, our findings agree with the latter study regarding the contribution of the pig farming system (pig farm + electricity supply) and the total feed supply to the total WD potential.|||| In this study, manure application to agricultural land was not considered in the system boundaries, in line with FAO (2018) for the case of treating manure as a residual. Thus, potentially avoided burdens for CC, acidification and eutrophication from mineral fer- tilizer production and land application were not considered. This could possibly be another explanation for the higher estimates in the current study for these EICIs. Finally, considering the impor- tance of feed ingredients' supply chains to all EICs (Fig. 2), the increased quantity of feed input per kg of pig live-weight in the systems of this study (CNVD: 3.48 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight; ATTBAD: 3.21 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight) in comparison to other studies (e.g. 2.46 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight in Noya et al (2017); 2.53 kg of feed/kg pig LW in Reckmann et al. (2013)) could explain the relatively increased EICI estimates in this work. Life Cycle Inventory data quality and uncertainty assess- ment. Data quality is discussed in the context of the pig farm- specific datasets developed for the CNVD and ATTBAD systems. The data quality indicators and levels were in agreement with EC-JRC (2010) and assigned in line with European Commis- sion (2018) in order to ensure consistency. The contribution analysis suggests that the pig farm-specific datasets are from a data quality point of view strongly affected by the secondary datasets (SeDs) associated with maize grain, barley grain, wheat bran, soybean meal and electricity supply as well as the activity data (AD) which determine their annual input quanti- ties to the pig farm. Furthermore, annual CH4, N20 and NH3 emis- sions (elementary flows, (EIFIs)) from the pig farm seem to be of importance. Given the fact that good time representativeness was ensured for the AD and the on-farm EIFIs and that SeDs were se- lected to this purpose, technological and geographical representa- tiveness as well as precision were the quality indicators of interest. Tables 8-10 respectively present data quality information for the AD, SeDs and EIFIs connected to the hot spots of the studied systems. The modifications applied in the original SeDs (Tables C1 and C2, Supplementary material - all processes except medium voltage electricity grid mix) generally rendered the resulting datasets more country-specific, improving the GeR level for the datasets used in the LCA models (in comparison to the level shown in Table 9). Tables 8-10 suggest a fair data quality level for the data quality indicators of the majority of AD, SeDs and EIFIs, and therefore fair data quality for the total LCI of both systems.||||

Nevertheless, data quality has a great potential for improve- ment. This could be achieved by developing country-specific LCI datasets for domestic cereal grains' cultivation and drying pro- cesses, cereal grain milling and oilseed crushing industrial pro-

cesses but also domestic market datasets which involve imports of feed components, in accordance with internationally accepted data quality guidelines (e.g. Fazio et al., 2019).

Monte Carlo analysis was finally performed as an approach to incorporate the uncertainty which is caused by the AD, SeD and farm level ElFls to the EICI results. In this purpose, the relevant SimaPro function was employed and a comparison between the CNVD and ATTBAD systems' EICI estimates was configured. The Monte Carlo stop criterion was set equal to 500 runs. Lognor- mal distributions were assumed for zootechnical parameters (apart from the performance parameters for fattening pigs in Table 6, for which a normal distribution was considered), for the nutri- ents of the various pig diets and for the manure properties. Pedi- gree matrices (e.g. Goedkoop et al., 2016) were completed for all these parameters in order to obtain geometrical standard devia- tions for their distributions. Normal, lognormal and triangle dis- tributions were considered for the emission estimation parameters (e.g. N retention, EFs etc.) which were taken from IPCC (2006a) and EEA (2016), exploiting the uncertainty information (e.g. ranges of estimates) provided in these documents. Pedigree matrices were finally modified for all the Ecoinvent V. 3.4. SeDs which were modified in this study and completed for the Greek market mix
datasets. The uncertainty analysis results are shown in Table 11 and refer to a 95% confidence interval.

Table 11 suggests significantly lower values for all the EICIs of the ATTBAD system in comparison to the CNVD system, with 95% confidence. It has to be noticed that not all sources and types of uncertainty in an ELCA (e.g. Finnveden et al., 2009) can be ad- dressed with such a Monte Carlo probabilistic simulation. However, these results could be used to further support the finding that the supply of the ATTBAD diet to fattening pigs could improve the en- vironmental performance of the studied system in comparison to the supply of the CNVD diet (Table 7).

and lower annual emission outputs per FU (Table 5). Nevertheless, the annual inputs of attapulgite and benzoic acid and their envi- ronmental impact per kg of supply were low (Tab. D1, Supplemen- tary Material) to negatively affect the environmental performance of the ATTBAD system in comparison to the CNVD system. Thus, the results of Table 4 can be explained.

The annual size of material inputs to the pig farm is a di- rect consequence of the FI in the fattening stage. The decreased FI (Table 6) in the ATTBAD system leads to decreased annual inputs of dried maize and barley grains, wheat bran, soybean meal and oil and mineral feed for fatteners. Moreover, the increased TWG of pigs when supplied the ATTBAD diet (Table 6) was connected to increased final live-weight per finisher and therefore to increased annual pig live-weight yield. This explains why even the inputs whose annual size was not influenced (i.e. herring meal, milk re- placer and sweet whey) or not considered to be influenced (i.e. water and electricity supply) by the diet modification, showed a lower value per FU in the ATTBAD system.

The lower estimates of CH4 from pigs' enteric fermentation and of PM emissions per FU in the ATTBAD system (Table 5) are also exclusively explained by the higher annual live-weight yield, as the Tier 1 approaches used for their calculation (EEA, 2016; IPCC, 2006a) employ constant EFs per head.

For the rest of the emissions, other parameters influencing their size per FU (apart from the annual live-weight yield), are the DE, the DM, the ash and the CP contents of the CNVD and ATTBAD. However, the difference in these parameters between the two di- ets is low (Table 1), implying small influence on the difference between the emissions' estimates. Concerning CH4 emission from OFSM, the total excretion of VS per fattening pig was highly in- fluenced by the total ration's intake (influence on total digestible energy, GE, urinary energy and Dry Matter Intake (DMI) per fat- tening pig - all larger in the CNVD system). Furthermore, larger ration consumption in the CNVD system was connected to larger total ration losses per fattening pig, influencing the quantity of VS in feed waste (larger in the CNVD system).

With regards to N emissions, the higher N intake per fatten- ing pig in the CNVD system was only dependent on their rations' intake. Moreover, the higher N retention per fattening pig in the ATTBAD system could be connected to its higher slaughter live- weight (ASAE, 2005). As an outcome, total N excretion was higher
in the CNVD system, leading to higher NH3, N20 and NO2 emis- sions from OFSM.
Finally, the estimation method of NMVOC emissions (EEA, 2016) indicated that the higher estimates in the CNVD system could be also explained by the total FI per fattening pig.

Contribution analysis and hot spots. Fig. 2 illustrates the % contribution of the supply of the various rations' ingredients and electricity as well as the pig farm to the EICIs assessed for the AT- TBAD system (Table 7).

It should be noticed that CNVD and ATTBAD systems presented similar behavior with respect to the way the various contributors influenced the EICI estimates. The % contribution differences be- tween the two systems were lower than 6% for all EICIs except for the indicator for fossil fuel depletion (ARDF) (12.5% higher contri- bution for the ATTBAD system). The % contribution of attapulgite and benzoic acid supplies was very low for all EICIs (<2.1%). The maximal contribution of benzoic acid supply was to ARDF (2.07%), explaining the aforementioned contribution difference for this EICI. The pig farm was the most important contributor to the CC (GWP- 100) mostly due to CH4 emission from the OFSM system (27.74% of the total estimate), acidification (AE-A (and AP)) and terrestrial eutrophication (AE-TE), exclusively due to the direct NH3 emis- sions from the farm buildings (PH) and the slurry storage facil- ity. In Fig. 2, the pig farm sub-system includes all the green- house gas (GHG) and pollutant emissions to air, estimated at the pig farm level (Table 5). Furthermore, maize grain supply was the most relevant for the marine eutrophication (FNMC), eutrophica- tion (EP) (mostly due to direct NO3 emissions to water bodies from the application of N fertilizers in domestic maize cultivation) and ARDF (mostly due to the electricity use in drying and irriga- tion in domestic maize production) while the barley grain supply for the freshwater eutrophication (FPFC), agricultural land occupa- tion (LOP) and water use (UDP). Finally, soybean meal supply was the most important contributor to the CC due to dLUC (GWP-100 (dLUC)).

Finally, considering the impor- tance of feed ingredients' supply chains to all EICs (Fig. 2), the increased quantity of feed input per kg of pig live-weight in the systems of this study (CNVD: 3.48 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight; ATTBAD: 3.21 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight) in comparison to other studies (e.g. 2.46 kg of feed/kg pig live-weight in Noya et al (2017); 2.53 kg of feed/kg pig LW in Reckmann et al. (2013)) could explain the relatively increased EICI estimates in this work. Life Cycle Inventory data quality and uncertainty assess- ment. Data quality is discussed in the context of the pig farm- specific datasets developed for the CNVD and ATTBAD systems. The data quality indicators and levels were in agreement with EC-JRC (2010) and assigned in line with European Commis- sion (2018) in order to ensure consistency. The contribution analysis suggests that the pig farm-specific datasets are from a data quality point of view strongly affected by the secondary datasets (SeDs) associated with maize grain, barley grain, wheat bran, soybean meal and electricity supply as well as the activity data (AD) which determine their annual input quanti- ties to the pig farm. Furthermore, annual CH4, N20 and NH3 emis- sions (elementary flows, (EIFIs)) from the pig farm seem to be of importance. Given the fact that good time representativeness was ensured for the AD and the on-farm EIFIs and that SeDs were se- lected to this purpose, technological and geographical representa- tiveness as well as precision were the quality indicators of interest. Tables 8-10 respectively present data quality information for the AD, SeDs and EIFIs connected to the hot spots of the studied systems. The modifications applied in the original SeDs (Tables C1 and C2, Supplementary material - all processes except medium voltage electricity grid mix) generally rendered the resulting datasets more country-specific, improving the GeR level for the datasets used in the LCA models (in comparison to the level shown in Table 9). Tables 8-10 suggest a fair data quality level for the data quality indicators of the majority of AD, SeDs and EIFIs, and therefore fair data quality for the total LCI of both systems.||||

Nevertheless, data quality has a great potential for improve- ment. This could be achieved by developing country-specific LCI datasets for domestic cereal grains' cultivation and drying pro- cesses, cereal grain milling and oilseed crushing industrial pro-

cesses but also domestic market datasets which involve imports of feed components, in accordance with internationally accepted data quality guidelines (e.g. Fazio et al., 2019).

Monte Carlo analysis was finally performed as an approach to incorporate the uncertainty which is caused by the AD, SeD and farm level ElFls to the EICI results. In this purpose, the relevant SimaPro function was employed and a comparison between the CNVD and ATTBAD systems' EICI estimates was configured. The Monte Carlo stop criterion was set equal to 500 runs. Lognor- mal distributions were assumed for zootechnical parameters (apart from the performance parameters for fattening pigs in Table 6, for which a normal distribution was considered), for the nutri- ents of the various pig diets and for the manure properties. Pedi- gree matrices (e.g. Goedkoop et al., 2016) were completed for all these parameters in order to obtain geometrical standard devia- tions for their distributions. Normal, lognormal and triangle dis- tributions were considered for the emission estimation parameters (e.g. N retention, EFs etc.) which were taken from IPCC (2006a) and EEA (2016), exploiting the uncertainty information (e.g. ranges of estimates) provided in these documents. Pedigree matrices were finally modified for all the Ecoinvent V. 3.4. SeDs which were modified in this study and completed for the Greek market mix
datasets. The uncertainty analysis results are shown in Table 11 and refer to a 95% confidence interval.

Table 11 suggests significantly lower values for all the EICIs of the ATTBAD system in comparison to the CNVD system, with 95% confidence. It has to be noticed that not all sources and types of uncertainty in an ELCA (e.g. Finnveden et al., 2009) can be ad- dressed with such a Monte Carlo probabilistic simulation. However, these results could be used to further support the finding that the supply of the ATTBAD diet to fattening pigs could improve the en- vironmental performance of the studied system in comparison to the supply of the CNVD diet (Table 7).
