cesses but also domestic market datasets which involve imports of feed components, in accordance with internationally accepted data quality guidelines (e.g. Fazio et al., 2019).

Monte Carlo analysis was finally performed as an approach to incorporate the uncertainty which is caused by the AD, SeD and farm level ElFls to the EICI results. In this purpose, the relevant SimaPro function was employed and a comparison between the CNVD and ATTBAD systems' EICI estimates was configured. The Monte Carlo stop criterion was set equal to 500 runs. Lognor- mal distributions were assumed for zootechnical parameters (apart from the performance parameters for fattening pigs in Table 6, for which a normal distribution was considered), for the nutri- ents of the various pig diets and for the manure properties. Pedi- gree matrices (e.g. Goedkoop et al., 2016) were completed for all these parameters in order to obtain geometrical standard devia- tions for their distributions. Normal, lognormal and triangle dis- tributions were considered for the emission estimation parameters (e.g. N retention, EFs etc.) which were taken from IPCC (2006a) and EEA (2016), exploiting the uncertainty information (e.g. ranges of estimates) provided in these documents. Pedigree matrices were finally modified for all the Ecoinvent V. 3.4. SeDs which were modified in this study and completed for the Greek market mix
datasets. The uncertainty analysis results are shown in Table 11 and refer to a 95% confidence interval.

Table 11 suggests significantly lower values for all the EICIs of the ATTBAD system in comparison to the CNVD system, with 95% confidence. It has to be noticed that not all sources and types of uncertainty in an ELCA (e.g. Finnveden et al., 2009) can be ad- dressed with such a Monte Carlo probabilistic simulation. However, these results could be used to further support the finding that the supply of the ATTBAD diet to fattening pigs could improve the en- vironmental performance of the studied system in comparison to the supply of the CNVD diet (Table 7).
22: The experimental part of this research suggested that addition of attapulgite and benzoic acid at the expense of maize in the diet of fattening pigs (ATTBAD) could enhance their growth and feed efficiency in comparison to the CNVD supplied by a commercial farrowing to finishing farm in Epirus Region, Greece. Although the existing literature provides potential explanation for this enhance- ment (improvement of intestinal functionality and enzyme activ- ity, increase in digesta retention time, drop of pH of digesta in the gastrointestinal tract), the experimental procedure performed

0: <title>V. Anestis, D.K. Papanastasiou and T. Bartzanas et al./Sustainable Production and Consumption 22 (2020) 162-176 </title>

in the current study was not specifically designed for investigat- ing the biological mechanisms which determine it. Thus, more re- search would be required in order to investigate these biological mechanisms and confirm the findings of this experimental proce- dure for fattening pigs, not only for the current experimental set up, but also for various attapulgite and benzoic acid levels as well as for various control diet compositions, taking in this way these two additional parameters into consideration. It should also be fur- ther investigated whether attapulgite exerts a positive or negative effect on benzoic acid's suggested mitigating activity on ammonia emissions (i.e. N retention and emission mechanism) from fatten- ing pigs' excreta. Finally, although it was not in the scope of the current research to elaborate economic effects, the findings could provide a positive indication towards the economic profitability of the pig farm of this case study, when supplying the ATTBAD diet instead of the CNVD diet. The ELCA indicated that per kg of pig live-weight at the farm gate, supply of the ATTBAD was connected to a potentially im- proved environmental performance (for all the impact categories assessed) in comparison to the CNVD. The environmental hot spots (i.e. most important contributing processes) for the systems de- fined by both diets and for all EICs were identical. These hots pots were on-farm emission processes (CH4, N20 and NH3 from the OFSM system) but also emission and resource-use processes re- lated to the cultivation of crops belonging to the supply chains of feed materials (maize grain, barley grain, soybean meal). The an- nual quantity of the latter flows at the farm level was reduced as a direct consequence of the reduction in the FI per finished pig when supplied the ATTBAD. The aforementioned emission flows and in- flows to the farm per kg pig live-weight were also reduced die to the increased market weight per finished pig, when supplied the ATTBAD diet. Thus, the improved feed efficiency and TWG in the fattening stage associated with the ATTBAD were decisive fac- tors for the potentially improved environmental performance of the produced pig live-weight from the farm when supplying this diet. Reasonable differences were found between the estimates of the EICIs of this research and the estimates of related studies in the literature. Moreover, the total LCI of the studied systems was char- acterized by fair data quality. Both the fact that an either sound explanation concerning the observed differences or an agreement with previous literature results exists and the fact that there is fair LCI data quality could be considered as good indications for the validity of the received LCA results. The performed Monte Carlo analysis could be used as an additional indication in this respect. Therefore, the argument for a potentially improved environmen- tal performance of the ATTBAD system could be supported. Nev- ertheless, more research is needed in Greece in order to develop country-specific LCI datasets for feed components, based on in- ternationally accepted data quality guidelines, in order to enhance data quality and credibility not only for the LCA results of pig pro- duction in Greece but also for the other livestock products in the country.
